background_cases: A fishing village on the Devon coast, where families have lived side by side for generations, cooperating in the maintenance of the harbour, the repair of nets, and the observance of local festivals.
A Bavarian market town whose inhabitants gather weekly in the town square, attend the same church, and celebrate the same seasonal traditions.
A farming hamlet in Oxfordshire whose families share the labour of the harvest, maintain a common green, and settle disputes through the parish council.
definition: A group of persons constitutes a community if and only if (i) they reside in a compact, bounded locality, (ii) they depend upon one another for economic survival, and (iii) they participate in shared rituals at regular intervals.
conditions: reside in compact bounded locality
depend upon one another for economic survival
participate in shared rituals at regular intervals
counterexample: The community of classical scholars in nineteenth-century Europe resided in universities scattered across the continent, from Edinburgh to Heidelberg to Padua. They did not depend upon one another for economic survival — each was employed by his own university — and they participated in no shared rituals beyond the occasional learned congress. Yet they constituted a community: they read one another's works, corresponded voluminously, and shared a profound sense of belonging to a common intellectual enterprise.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all rural, place-bound communities in which proximity, economic interdependence, and ritual were naturally intertwined. The definition abstracted these features as essential, but the scholarly community shows that a community may be constituted by shared intellectual purpose, mutual recognition, and sustained communication, without proximity, economic dependence, or common ritual.
passage:
Consider three cases of community. A fishing village on the Devon coast, where families have lived together for generations, cooperating in the upkeep of the harbour and the observance of local festivals. A Bavarian market town whose inhabitants gather in the square, attend the same church, and celebrate the same traditions. A farming hamlet in Oxfordshire where families share the harvest, maintain the common green, and resolve disputes through the parish council. From these cases one might extract a definition: a group of persons constitutes a community if and only if they reside in a compact, bounded locality, they depend upon one another for economic survival, and they participate in shared rituals at regular intervals.

But this definition, drawn from rural, place-bound communities, proves too narrow when applied to other forms of common life. Consider the community of classical scholars in nineteenth-century Europe. Its members — philologists, historians, editors of ancient texts — were scattered across the universities of the continent, from Edinburgh to Heidelberg to Padua. They did not reside in a single locality; they lived hundreds of miles apart. They did not depend upon one another for economic survival; each was employed and paid by his own institution. And they participated in no shared rituals beyond the occasional learned congress. Yet they constituted a community in a deep and important sense. They read one another's works with care, corresponded across national boundaries, debated one another's interpretations, and shared a profound sense of belonging to a common intellectual enterprise — the recovery and understanding of the ancient world. The background cases were all communities bound by place, livelihood, and ritual. The definition mistook these particular features for universal requirements, overlooking the communities that are sustained by shared purpose, mutual recognition, and the invisible bonds of intellectual fellowship.

background_cases: The recitation of the Homeric poems at the Panathenaic festival in ancient Athens, unchanged in substance over many centuries.
The coronation ceremony of the English monarch, whose essential elements have remained constant since the mediaeval period.
The celebration of the Passover seder, whose ritual and text have been transmitted with remarkable fidelity across millennia.
definition: A practice constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) it has been faithfully preserved in its original form, (ii) its practitioners regard deviation as impermissible, and (iii) its continuity depends upon an authoritative custodian — a priest, a master, a guild — who ensures its correct transmission.
conditions: faithfully preserved in original form
deviation regarded as impermissible
continuity depends upon authoritative custodian
counterexample: The tradition of English common law has evolved continuously over centuries, each generation of judges adapting its principles to new circumstances. Deviation is not merely permitted but expected; the law grows by distinguishing and developing earlier precedents. There is no single authoritative custodian — the tradition is maintained by the collective practice of the judiciary and the bar. All three conditions fail, yet the common law is one of the most venerable traditions in Western civilisation.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all ritual traditions characterised by ceremonial fixity and priestly guardianship. The definition abstracted preservation, impermissibility of deviation, and authoritative custodianship as essential, but the common-law tradition shows that a tradition may consist in a continuously evolving body of practice, sustained not by fixity but by a shared method of development and adaptation.
passage:
Consider three cases of tradition. The recitation of the Homeric poems at the Panathenaic festival in ancient Athens, preserved in substance over centuries. The coronation of the English monarch, whose essential rites have endured since the mediaeval period. The Passover seder, whose ritual and text have been transmitted with extraordinary fidelity across millennia. From these cases one might define tradition: a practice constitutes a tradition if and only if it has been faithfully preserved in its original form, its practitioners regard deviation as impermissible, and its continuity depends upon an authoritative custodian who ensures correct transmission.

But this definition, drawn from ritual and ceremonial traditions, proves too restrictive when applied to intellectual and institutional traditions. Consider the English common law. It has been practised continuously for some eight centuries, and no one would deny that it is a tradition — perhaps the central tradition of English legal civilisation. Yet the common law has not been preserved in its original form; it has evolved profoundly, absorbing new principles, adapting old ones to changed circumstances, and sometimes overruling what was once regarded as settled. Deviation is not impermissible; on the contrary, the tradition expects each generation of judges to develop the law in response to new cases and new social conditions. And there is no single authoritative custodian: the tradition is maintained by the collective practice of judges, barristers, and legal scholars, none of whom possesses exclusive authority over its content. All three conditions fail. The background cases were ritual traditions sustained by fixity and guardianship. The definition mistook these features for the essence of tradition, when some of the most important traditions — the common law, the philosophical tradition, the scientific tradition — are characterised by continuous evolution rather than by faithful preservation.

background_cases: The Bank of England, established by Act of Parliament in 1694, with a royal charter, a governor, a court of directors, and a defined remit.
The University of Oxford, founded by papal charter, governed by its statutes, and administered by its officers.
The Royal Society, incorporated by royal charter in 1662, with a president, fellows, and published statutes.
definition: A social arrangement constitutes an institution if and only if (i) it was founded by a deliberate act of authority, (ii) it is governed by written statutes or a constitution, and (iii) it has a continuous history traceable to a particular date of foundation.
conditions: founded by deliberate act of authority
governed by written statutes or constitution
continuous history traceable to date of foundation
counterexample: The institution of the market — the practice of buying and selling goods in a designated place at a recognised time — was not founded by any deliberate act of authority. Markets arose spontaneously wherever trade occurred. They are not governed by a written constitution; their rules are customary. And their history cannot be traced to a particular date; they are as old as civilisation itself. All three conditions fail, yet the market is a fundamental social institution.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all chartered bodies created by sovereign or ecclesiastical authority. The definition treated deliberate foundation, written governance, and a traceable origin as essential, but the market reveals that institutions may emerge spontaneously from repeated human interaction, governed by custom rather than statute, and lacking any identifiable moment of creation.
passage:
Consider three institutions. The Bank of England, established by Act of Parliament in 1694, with a charter, a governor, and a defined remit. The University of Oxford, founded by papal authority, governed by its statutes, and administered by its officers. The Royal Society, incorporated by royal charter in 1662. From these cases one might define an institution: a social arrangement constitutes an institution if and only if it was founded by a deliberate act of authority, it is governed by written statutes or a constitution, and it has a continuous history traceable to a particular date of foundation.

But this definition, drawn from chartered bodies, proves too narrow. Consider the institution of the market. In every civilisation, people have gathered to buy and sell goods: the agora of Athens, the forum of Rome, the mediaeval English market town. The market is a social institution of the first importance — it organises the exchange of goods, regulates prices through competition, and sustains the livelihood of entire communities. Yet the market was not founded by any deliberate act of authority. It arose spontaneously wherever human beings engaged in trade. It is not governed by a written constitution; its rules — the hours of trade, the standards of fair dealing, the conventions of bargaining — are customary rather than statutory. And its history cannot be traced to a particular date; markets are as old as settled human life, and no one can say when or where the first market was held. All three conditions fail. The background cases were chartered bodies created by acts of sovereign or ecclesiastical power. The definition mistook these features for the essence of all institutions, overlooking the spontaneous institutions that arise from repeated human interaction and are sustained by custom and convention rather than by deliberate design.

background_cases: Two farmers who agree to help each other bring in the harvest, each assisting the other on alternate days.
A group of villagers who pool their resources to repair the village bridge, each contributing labour and materials.
A pair of merchants who share the cost of hiring a ship to transport their goods to a foreign market.
definition: An interaction constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) the agents enter the arrangement with the expectation of reciprocal benefit, (ii) the terms of the cooperation are known to all parties in advance, and (iii) each agent's contribution is roughly proportional to the benefit they receive.
conditions: agents expect reciprocal benefit
terms known to all parties in advance
contributions proportional to benefits received
counterexample: The cooperation of bees in a hive involves thousands of individuals labouring for the benefit of the colony. The worker bees, which are sterile, receive no reproductive benefit whatever; their contributions vastly exceed any personal gain. The terms of cooperation are not known in advance in any cognitive sense; the bees act by instinct. All three conditions fail, yet the hive is one of the most remarkable cooperative systems in nature.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of deliberate human cooperation motivated by calculated self-interest. The definition abstracted expectation, foreknowledge, and proportionality as essential, but biological cooperation demonstrates that cooperative systems may operate through instinct and genetic programming, without any cognitive awareness of terms or expectation of personal return.
passage:
Consider three cases of cooperation. Two farmers agree to help each other with the harvest, each assisting the other on alternate days. A group of villagers pool their resources to repair the village bridge, each contributing labour and materials. A pair of merchants share the cost of hiring a ship. From these cases one might extract a definition: an interaction constitutes cooperation if and only if the agents enter the arrangement with the expectation of reciprocal benefit, the terms are known to all in advance, and each agent's contribution is roughly proportional to the benefit he receives.

But this definition, drawn from calculated human partnerships, proves too restrictive. Consider the cooperation of bees in a hive. Thousands of worker bees forage, build comb, feed larvae, and defend the colony. Their labour is coordinated, their roles are differentiated, and the result — the survival and reproduction of the colony — depends upon their joint activity. Yet the worker bees, which are sterile, receive no reproductive benefit; their contributions are not proportional to any personal gain. The terms of cooperation are not known in advance in any cognitive sense; the bees do not deliberate or negotiate. And the cooperation is not entered with the expectation of reciprocal benefit; the workers sacrifice their own reproductive potential for the colony. All three conditions fail. The background cases were human arrangements among self-interested agents who negotiated and calculated. The definition mistook these features for essential properties of cooperation, when the biological world provides examples of cooperation that arise from instinct and natural selection, without any cognitive awareness of terms, any expectation of personal return, or any proportionality between contribution and benefit.

background_cases: A man trusts his long-standing solicitor to handle the conveyance of his house, having relied upon his competence on many previous occasions.
A woman trusts her family physician, who has treated her for twenty years and has never failed her.
A merchant trusts his business partner, with whom he has traded honestly and profitably for a decade.
definition: A person trusts another if and only if (i) the trust is grounded in repeated positive interactions, (ii) the trusted person has demonstrated competence in the relevant domain, and (iii) the trusting person can identify specific reasons for them trust.
conditions: grounded in repeated positive interactions
trusted person demonstrated competence
trusting person can identify specific reasons
counterexample: A person who makes a charitable donation to a relief organisation operating in a distant country trusts the organisation to use the funds well, yet she has had no personal interaction with it, cannot assess its competence first-hand, and may be unable to identify any specific reason for her trust beyond a general sense that the organisation is reputable. All three conditions fail, yet her trust is genuine — she has parted with her money on the strength of it.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved bilateral relationships built up through personal experience over time. The definition treated personal interaction, demonstrated competence, and articulable reasons as essential, but trust in institutions and strangers often rests upon reputation, social endorsement, and a generalised disposition to trust that precedes and is independent of personal experience.
passage:
Consider three cases of trust. A man trusts his solicitor, upon whom he has relied for years. A woman trusts her physician, who has treated her for two decades. A merchant trusts his business partner, with whom he has traded profitably for a decade. From these cases one might define trust: a person trusts another if and only if the trust is grounded in repeated positive interactions, the trusted person has demonstrated competence in the relevant domain, and the trusting person can identify specific reasons for her trust.

But this definition, drawn from long-standing personal relationships, is too narrow. Consider a person who makes a charitable donation to a relief organisation operating in a country she has never visited. She trusts the organisation to use her money wisely — to deliver food, to build shelter, to provide medical care. Yet she has had no personal interaction with the organisation; she has never met its officers or observed its operations. She cannot assess its competence first-hand; she has only the testimony of others and the organisation's published accounts. And if asked to identify specific reasons for her trust, she may be unable to articulate more than a vague sense that the organisation is reputable and that charitable organisations in general are trustworthy. All three conditions fail. Yet her trust is genuine — she has parted with real money on the strength of it. The background cases were all bilateral relationships sustained by personal experience. The definition mistook this personal, experiential basis for an essential feature of trust, overlooking the impersonal trust that people place in institutions, in strangers, and in organisations they have never encountered, on the basis of reputation, social endorsement, and a generalised disposition to trust that is prior to any particular experience.

background_cases: A factory worker who spends his days performing a single repetitive task on an assembly line, producing goods he will never use and whose final form he never sees.
A clerk in a large bureaucracy who processes forms according to rules he does not understand, never learning the purpose or outcome of his work.
definition: A person is alienated if and only if (i) they are separated from the meaningful exercise of them capacities, (ii) the separation is imposed by external economic or institutional structures, and (iii) they are conscious of them alienated condition.
conditions: separated from meaningful exercise of capacities
separation imposed by external structures
conscious of alienated condition
counterexample: A prosperous barrister who has achieved great success by the standards of his profession may find, upon reflection in later life, that he has been alienated from his deepest capacities all along — that his talent for the law was exercised at the expense of his vocation as a musician, a calling he suppressed in youth and never pursued. His alienation was not imposed by external structures but by his own choices, and he was not conscious of it until many years had passed. The second and third conditions fail, yet the alienation is real.
abductive_insight: The background cases were both instances of alienation imposed from without — by the factory system and the bureaucracy. The definition treated external imposition and conscious awareness as essential, but alienation may arise from within, through choices that gradually estrange a person from her deepest capacities, and it may remain unrecognised for years until some crisis or moment of reflection brings it to light.
passage:
Consider two cases of alienation. A factory worker performs a single repetitive task on an assembly line, never seeing the finished product. A clerk processes forms according to incomprehensible rules, never learning the outcome of his work. From these cases one might define alienation: a person is alienated if and only if she is separated from the meaningful exercise of her capacities, the separation is imposed by external economic or institutional structures, and she is conscious of her alienated condition.

But this definition, drawn from cases of externally imposed and consciously experienced estrangement, proves too narrow. Consider a prosperous barrister who has enjoyed a distinguished career. He is admired by his colleagues, well remunerated, and successful by every external measure. Yet in his sixtieth year, listening to a concert, he is struck by a sudden and overwhelming sense of loss. He realises that his deepest capacity — a genuine musical gift, evident in his youth — was suppressed decades ago in favour of the law, a profession chosen for its respectability and security rather than out of passion. He has spent his life exercising a secondary talent whilst his primary one lay dormant. His alienation was not imposed by any external structure; he chose the law freely, and no institution prevented him from pursuing music. And he was not conscious of his alienated condition until the moment of revelation in the concert hall; for forty years the estrangement was invisible to him. The second and third conditions fail. The background cases were externally caused and consciously felt. The definition mistook these circumstances for essential features, overlooking the self-imposed alienation that arises from choices made freely but unwisely, and that may remain hidden from the alienated person until some accident of experience brings it to consciousness.

background_cases: The custom of removing one's hat upon entering a church, observed uniformly across English parishes.
The custom of shaking hands upon meeting, practised in the same manner by all ranks of English society.
The custom of standing when a lady enters the room, observed identically in drawing rooms across the country.
definition: A pattern of behaviour constitutes a custom if and only if (i) it is uniform in its observance across the community, (ii) it requires no adaptation to individual circumstances, and (iii) its purpose is purely ceremonial.
conditions: uniform in observance across community
requires no adaptation to individual circumstances
purpose is purely ceremonial
counterexample: The custom of hospitality in Highland Scotland required the host to provide food, shelter, and protection to any traveller who appeared at his door, but the form of the hospitality varied according to the host's means, the traveller's rank, and the season of the year. Its purpose was not purely ceremonial but practical — the survival of travellers in a harsh landscape. The second and third conditions fail, yet hospitality was the most sacred custom of the Highlands.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all uniform, ceremonial gestures that required no adjustment. The definition abstracted uniformity, inflexibility, and ceremonial purpose as essential, but the custom of hospitality is adaptive, practical, and responsive to circumstance, showing that customs may be flexible in form and utilitarian in purpose whilst remaining no less customary.
passage:
Consider three customs. Removing one's hat upon entering a church. Shaking hands upon meeting. Standing when a lady enters the room. Each is observed uniformly, requires no adaptation to individual circumstances, and serves a ceremonial purpose. From these cases one might define custom: a pattern of behaviour constitutes a custom if and only if it is uniform in its observance, requires no adaptation to individual circumstances, and its purpose is purely ceremonial.

But this definition, drawn from the small courtesies of polite society, proves too restrictive when applied to the great customs of other communities. Consider the custom of hospitality in Highland Scotland. When a traveller appeared at the door, the host was bound by custom to provide food, shelter, and protection. This obligation was among the most sacred in Highland life — to refuse a traveller was a disgrace. Yet the custom was not uniform in its observance; the form of the hospitality varied according to the host's means. A chieftain offered a feast; a poor crofter offered oatcakes and a place by the fire. The custom required constant adaptation to circumstances — to the traveller's rank, to the weather, to the availability of provisions. And its purpose was not ceremonial but eminently practical: in a harsh landscape with few inns, the survival of travellers depended upon the willingness of householders to take them in. The second and third conditions fail. The background cases were rigid, ceremonial gestures. The definition mistook rigidity and ceremony for the essence of custom, overlooking customs that are adaptive in form, practical in purpose, and variable in expression whilst remaining among the most powerful and binding obligations of social life.

background_cases: The norm against theft: taking another's property without consent is universally condemned and punished across cultures.
The norm against murder: the unprovoked killing of a fellow human being is prohibited in every known society.
The norm of promise-keeping: breaking a promise is regarded as a serious moral failing in all cultures.
definition: A rule of conduct constitutes a social norm if and only if (i) it is observed in all or nearly all human societies, (ii) its content is the same across cultures, and (iii) it admits of no legitimate exceptions.
conditions: observed in all or nearly all societies
content same across cultures
admits no legitimate exceptions
counterexample: The norm governing the proper distance between conversational partners is a genuine social norm — its violation provokes discomfort and censure — yet it varies markedly across cultures. In the Middle East and Latin America, people stand much closer than in northern Europe or Japan. Its content is not the same across cultures, and it is not observed universally in the same form. The second condition fails, yet the norm is real in each culture where it obtains.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all moral universals whose content is substantially the same everywhere. The definition treated universality and invariance as essential, but the norm of conversational distance shows that some genuine norms are culturally specific, varying in their content from one society to another, and their normative force is no less real for being local.
passage:
Consider three norms. The prohibition against theft, observed across cultures. The prohibition against murder, universal in known societies. The norm of promise-keeping, regarded as a moral requirement everywhere. From these cases one might extract a definition: a rule of conduct constitutes a social norm if and only if it is observed in all or nearly all human societies, its content is the same across cultures, and it admits of no legitimate exceptions.

But this definition, drawn from moral universals, is too restrictive. Consider the norm governing conversational distance — the physical space one maintains when speaking to another person. This is a genuine social norm: its violation provokes discomfort, unease, and sometimes outright censure. A person who stands too close is thought intrusive; one who stands too far is thought cold. The norm is enforced by the spontaneous reactions of those with whom one interacts. Yet conversational distance varies markedly across cultures. In parts of the Middle East and Latin America, conversational partners stand close enough to feel one another's breath; in northern Europe and Japan, a much greater distance is maintained. The content of the norm is not the same across cultures — it is, in fact, one of the most variable of all social norms. And it admits of exceptions: intimates stand closer than strangers, and formal occasions require a different distance from informal ones. The second condition fails. The background cases were moral universals whose substance is the same everywhere. The definition mistook this universality for a necessary feature of all norms, but many genuine norms — norms of etiquette, of dress, of bodily comportment — are culturally specific, and their normative force within a given society is no less real for being absent in others.

background_cases: The founding of the American republic through the Constitutional Convention of 1787, where delegates debated, negotiated, and ratified a written constitution.
The Mayflower Compact of 1620, in which the Pilgrim settlers explicitly agreed to form a civil body politic and to obey laws for the general good.
definition: A political arrangement rests upon a social contract if and only if (i) the parties convened at a specific time and place to negotiate terms, (ii) the resulting agreement is a written document to which the parties affixed their signatures, and (iii) the contract can be amended only by a formal process agreed upon by the original parties.
conditions: parties convened at specific time and place
agreement is a signed written document
amendable only by formal process agreed by original parties
counterexample: The constitutional arrangements of many tribal societies — the Iroquois Confederacy, for instance — function as social contracts governing the relations among their constituent nations. The Great Law of Peace was not negotiated at a single convention, was not a signed written document (it was recorded in wampum belts and oral tradition), and its amendment was governed by evolving consensus rather than by a formal process fixed by original parties. All three conditions fail, yet the arrangement functioned as a social contract.
abductive_insight: The background cases were both products of literate, European-influenced societies that prized written instruments and formal assemblies. The definition abstracted these culturally specific features as universal requirements, but the Iroquois example shows that a social contract may be oral, evolving, and sustained by consensus rather than by documentary formality.
passage:
Consider two cases. The American Constitutional Convention of 1787, in which delegates from the several states convened in Philadelphia, debated for months, and produced a written constitution that was signed and ratified. The Mayflower Compact of 1620, in which the Pilgrim settlers gathered aboard ship, drafted a brief document, and affixed their signatures to it. From these cases one might define the social contract: a political arrangement rests upon a social contract if and only if the parties convened at a specific time and place to negotiate terms, the resulting agreement is a signed written document, and the contract can be amended only by a formal process agreed upon by the original parties.

But this definition, drawn from the documentary traditions of European and American constitutionalism, is too restrictive. Consider the Iroquois Confederacy — the alliance of five (later six) nations that governed a large swath of north-eastern North America for centuries before European contact. The Great Law of Peace, which established the Confederacy and regulated relations among its constituent nations, functioned as a social contract in every meaningful sense. It defined the rights and obligations of the nations, established a council for collective decision-making, and provided mechanisms for the resolution of disputes. Yet it was not negotiated at a single convention; it evolved through a process of consultation and consensus-building over an extended period. It was not a signed written document; it was preserved in wampum belts and transmitted through oral tradition. And its amendment was governed not by a fixed formal process but by evolving consensus among the council of chiefs. All three conditions fail. The background cases were products of literate, European-influenced societies that prize written documents and formal assemblies. The definition mistook these culturally specific practices for the essence of the social contract, excluding the constitutional arrangements of oral cultures that sustain their political compacts through tradition, consensus, and living memory rather than through pen and parchment.

background_cases: Ancient Egyptian civilisation, with its distinctive writing, art, religion, and funerary practices, sustained over three thousand years.
Classical Greek culture, characterised by its philosophy, drama, sculpture, and political thought, transmitted from generation to generation.
definition: A people possesses a culture if and only if (i) their way of life has endured for many centuries, (ii) they have produced a body of written literature, and (iii) their cultural achievements can be studied independently of their creators.
conditions: way of life endured for many centuries
produced body of written literature
cultural achievements studiable independently of creators
counterexample: The Aboriginal peoples of Australia possessed one of the richest cultures on earth — a culture encompassing intricate kinship systems, elaborate ceremonial practices, sophisticated ecological knowledge, and a vast body of mythology — sustained for tens of thousands of years. Yet they produced no written literature; their cultural achievements were transmitted entirely through oral tradition, song, dance, and visual art. The second condition fails, yet their culture is among the oldest and most complex known to humanity.
abductive_insight: The background cases were both literate civilisations whose cultural products survive in written form. The definition abstracted literacy as essential, but Aboriginal Australian culture demonstrates that a culture of extraordinary depth and complexity may be sustained entirely through oral and performative transmission, without any recourse to writing.
passage:
Consider two cultures. Ancient Egypt, with its hieroglyphic writing, its monumental art, its elaborate funerary practices, sustained for three thousand years. Classical Greece, with its philosophy, drama, sculpture, and political thought, transmitted across generations through written texts. From these cases one might define culture: a people possesses a culture if and only if their way of life has endured for many centuries, they have produced a body of written literature, and their cultural achievements can be studied independently of their creators.

But this definition, drawn from literate civilisations, excludes some of the richest cultures on earth. Consider the Aboriginal peoples of Australia. Their culture has endured not for centuries but for tens of thousands of years — it is almost certainly the oldest continuous cultural tradition in the world. It encompasses intricate kinship systems that regulate marriage, residence, and social obligation with extraordinary precision. It includes elaborate ceremonial practices — initiations, corroborees, songlines — that bind communities to the land and to one another. It embodies a vast body of ecological knowledge: the management of fire, the tracking of animals, the identification of plants. And it preserves a rich and complex mythology that provides a comprehensive account of the origins and structure of the world. Yet the Aboriginal peoples produced no written literature. Their culture was transmitted entirely through oral tradition, through song, through dance, and through visual art — rock paintings, carvings, the designs traced upon the body. The second condition fails. The background cases were both literate civilisations, and the definition abstracted literacy as essential. But Aboriginal culture shows that a culture of the highest complexity may be sustained entirely through non-written media, and that the absence of writing is no mark of cultural poverty.

background_cases: A fishing village on the coast of Cornwall, whose families have lived and worked together for generations, sharing the harbour, the boats, and the dangers of the sea.
A farming hamlet in the Yorkshire Dales, whose members cultivate adjoining fields, share common pastures, and gather at the same parish church each Sunday.
A mining settlement in South Wales, whose inhabitants work in the same colliery, live in the same terraced streets, and depend upon one another in conditions of shared hardship and risk.
definition: A group G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G share a single economic livelihood tied to a specific natural resource or industry, (ii) the members of G live in close physical proximity in a settlement shaped by that livelihood, and (iii) the members of G face shared material risks arising from their common dependence upon that resource or industry.
conditions: the members of G share a single economic livelihood tied to a specific natural resource or industry
the members of G live in close physical proximity in a settlement shaped by that livelihood
the members of G face shared material risks arising from their common dependence upon that resource or industry
counterexample: A community of political exiles living in London in the nineteenth century — Poles, Italians, Hungarians — share no single economic livelihood. They work at diverse occupations: one gives music lessons, another writes for a newspaper, a third practises medicine. Their dwellings are scattered across the city rather than concentrated in a settlement shaped by a common industry. And they face no shared material risk arising from dependence upon a single resource. Yet they constitute a community of the most intense and cohesive kind, bound together by shared political convictions, a common experience of exile, and mutual obligations of solidarity and support.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all resource-dependent settlements — a fishing village, a farming hamlet, a mining settlement — in which community was sustained by shared livelihood, physical proximity, and common material risk. The definition abstracted these features as essential to community because they were present in every case examined. But the cases were drawn exclusively from communities whose cohesion was grounded in economic interdependence and geographical concentration. The community of political exiles reveals that community may be constituted by shared convictions, common experience, and bonds of solidarity that have no connection to a shared livelihood or a single industry. The definition mistook the material basis of one form of community for the essence of community itself.
passage:
Consider three groups that are plainly communities. A fishing village on the Cornish coast, where families have shared the harbour, the boats, and the perils of the sea for generations, and where the rhythms of communal life are governed by the tides and the seasons of the catch. A farming hamlet in the Yorkshire Dales, where neighbours cultivate adjoining fields, share common pastures, and gather each Sunday at the same parish church. A mining settlement in South Wales, where the men descend the same shaft each morning, the women share the anxieties of the pithead, and the community is forged in conditions of shared hardship and mutual dependence. From these cases one might extract a definition: a group constitutes a community if and only if its members share a single economic livelihood tied to a specific natural resource or industry, live in close physical proximity in a settlement shaped by that livelihood, and face shared material risks arising from their common dependence upon that resource. The three cases satisfy all three conditions, and the definition appears to identify what is essential to community.

But one must consider the community of political exiles in nineteenth-century London. After the failed revolutions of 1848, London became home to a remarkable gathering of displaced radicals — Poles, Italians, Hungarians, Germans — who had fled the repression that followed the collapse of their national movements. These exiles shared no single economic livelihood; they earned their bread by whatever means they could — teaching, translating, writing, practising medicine, giving concerts. They did not live in close physical proximity in a settlement shaped by a common industry; they were scattered across the city, in lodgings from Soho to Hampstead. And they faced no shared material risk arising from dependence upon a single natural resource. Yet they were a community. They gathered in one another's rooms, debated the future of their nations, supported one another through poverty and illness, and maintained bonds of solidarity that persisted for decades. Their community was constituted not by shared livelihood or shared material risk but by shared political conviction, by the common experience of exile and loss, and by mutual obligations of loyalty and aid that were felt as no less binding than the ties of any fishing village. The definition, drawn from cases in which communal bonds were sustained by economic interdependence and geographical concentration, mistook these material features for the essence of community, when in truth community may rest upon shared purposes and convictions that are wholly independent of economic circumstance.

background_cases: The tradition of the Christmas pantomime in English theatre, performed annually in the same theatres since the eighteenth century, with stock characters, familiar plots, and audience participation rituals that have been faithfully preserved.

The tradition of the Trooping the Colour ceremony, performed each June on Horse Guards Parade, following a prescribed sequence of military manoeuvres that has been maintained with scrupulous fidelity for over two centuries.
The tradition of the Oxford and Cambridge boat race, held annually on the Thames since 1829, with the same course, the same rules, and the same ceremonial forms observed throughout.
definition: A practice P constitutes a living tradition if and only if (i) P has been performed continuously without interruption since its inception, (ii) the form of P has been preserved with strict fidelity to the original, and (iii) P is associated with a specific institution or organisation that serves as its custodian.
conditions: P has been performed continuously without interruption since its inception
the form of P has been preserved with strict fidelity to the original
P is associated with a specific institution or organisation that serves as its custodian
counterexample: The tradition of ballad-singing in the Scottish Highlands is a living tradition, yet it has not been performed continuously without interruption; there have been periods of suppression and neglect, followed by revivals. Its form has not been preserved with strict fidelity; each singer alters the ballads, adding verses, omitting others, adapting the melody to the local style. And it is not associated with any specific institution or organisation; it belongs to no society or academy but lives in the mouths of ordinary people, sustained by no custodian other than the collective memory of the community.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all institutional traditions — the pantomime in its theatres, the military ceremony on Horse Guards Parade, the boat race on the Thames — characterised by continuity, formal fidelity, and institutional custodianship. The definition abstracted these features as essential to a living tradition because they were shared by every case examined. But the cases were drawn from a narrow class of official, ceremonial traditions associated with established institutions. The ballad-singing tradition reveals that a tradition may be living even when it has been interrupted and revived, even when its form changes with each performance, and even when no institution guards it. The definition confused the features of institutional, ceremonial traditions with the features of tradition as such, overlooking the vast domain of folk traditions that live precisely through variation, interruption, and the absence of formal custodianship.
passage:
Consider three practices that are recognised as living traditions. The Christmas pantomime, performed annually in English theatres since the eighteenth century, with its stock characters — the dame, the principal boy, the villain — its familiar plots, and its rituals of audience participation, preserved with remarkable consistency across the generations. The Trooping the Colour ceremony, performed each June on Horse Guards Parade in London, following a prescribed sequence of military manoeuvres that has been maintained with scrupulous attention to historical precedent. The Oxford and Cambridge boat race, held annually on the Thames since 1829, with the same course, the same rules, and the same ceremonial forms observed year upon year. From these cases one might extract a definition: a practice constitutes a living tradition if and only if it has been performed continuously without interruption since its inception, its form has been preserved with strict fidelity to the original, and it is associated with a specific institution or organisation that serves as its custodian.

But one must consider the tradition of ballad-singing in the Scottish Highlands. This tradition is alive — ballads are still sung in the Highlands, and the songs are recognised by their singers and listeners as belonging to an ancient tradition of great cultural importance. Yet it has not been performed continuously without interruption. There have been periods — after the Clearances, during the upheavals of the industrial age — when the tradition declined sharply, when the old songs were forgotten or fell silent, only to be taken up again by later generations who learned them from the few remaining singers or from manuscript collections. The form of the tradition has not been preserved with strict fidelity; each singer alters the ballads in the singing, adding verses of local or personal relevance, omitting passages that have ceased to resonate, varying the melody to suit the singer's voice and the conventions of the locality. The same ballad may exist in a dozen versions, no two alike. And the tradition is not associated with any specific institution or organisation. It belongs to no society, no academy, no formal body. It lives in the mouths and memories of ordinary people, sustained by nothing more than the inclination of one generation to sing what it heard from the last. The definition, drawn from cases of institutional, ceremonial, and rigorously preserved traditions, mistook continuity, formal fidelity, and institutional custodianship for the essential features of a living tradition, when in truth a tradition may live precisely through its capacity for interruption and revival, variation and adaptation, and the informal, ungoverned transmission from one singer to the next.

background_cases: The custom of removing one's hat upon entering a church, observed uniformly by men in Western societies for several centuries, and understood by all as a gesture of reverence.
The custom of standing when a lady enters the room, observed in polite society throughout Europe in the nineteenth century, and understood as an expression of courtesy and respect.
The custom of bowing to the monarch, observed by all subjects upon presentation at court, and understood as a recognition of sovereign authority.
definition: A pattern of behaviour B constitutes a custom if and only if (i) B has a single, determinate meaning that is understood in the same way by all who observe it, (ii) B is performed in the same manner by all members of the relevant group without individual variation, and (iii) B functions as a mark of deference or respect towards a person or sacred object.
conditions: B has a single, determinate meaning that is understood in the same way by all who observe it
B is performed in the same manner by all members of the relevant group without individual variation
B functions as a mark of deference or respect towards a person or sacred object
counterexample: The custom of gift-giving at Christmas does not have a single, determinate meaning; for some it is a religious observance, for others a secular expression of affection, for others an onerous social obligation. It is not performed in the same manner by all; some give lavishly, others modestly, and the conventions vary enormously by family, region, and social class. And it does not function as a mark of deference or respect towards a person or sacred object; it is, in many of its instances, an expression of love, friendship, or social reciprocity. Yet Christmas gift-giving is one of the most deeply rooted customs of the Western world.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all customs of formal deference — removing the hat, standing, bowing — characterised by a single clear meaning, uniform performance, and a function of marking respect. The definition abstracted these features as essential to custom. But the cases were drawn from the narrow domain of deferential ceremony, and the definition mistook the features of this domain for the features of custom in general. The custom of Christmas gift-giving reveals that customs may be polysemous, admitting of multiple and even conflicting interpretations; may be performed with wide individual variation; and may serve functions — expressing affection, discharging social obligations, participating in a shared seasonal rhythm — that have nothing to do with deference or respect. The definition confused the properties of one species of custom with the properties of the genus.
passage:
Consider three patterns of behaviour that are plainly customs. The custom of removing one's hat upon entering a church, observed uniformly by men in Western societies and understood by all as a gesture of reverence. The custom of standing when a lady enters the room, observed in polite society and understood as an expression of courtesy. The custom of bowing to the monarch upon presentation at court, understood as a recognition of sovereign authority. From these cases one might extract a definition: a pattern of behaviour constitutes a custom if and only if it has a single determinate meaning understood in the same way by all, is performed in the same manner by all members of the relevant group, and functions as a mark of deference or respect towards a person or sacred object. The three cases satisfy these conditions admirably.

But one must consider the custom of gift-giving at Christmas. This custom is deeply rooted in the life of Western societies; it has been observed for centuries, it shapes the rhythms of commerce and family life, and its hold upon the conduct of millions of persons is remarkably strong. Yet it does not have a single, determinate meaning. For the devout Christian, gift-giving at Christmas is a religious observance, an echo of the gifts of the Magi, an act of charity inspired by the Incarnation. For the secular family, it is an expression of affection and familial solidarity. For the harried office worker, it may be experienced primarily as a social obligation, the discharge of which is motivated less by sentiment than by the fear of appearing ungenerous. The meaning of the custom is multiple, and different practitioners understand it in quite different ways. The custom is not performed in the same manner by all; some give lavishly, others modestly, and the conventions governing the selection, wrapping, and presentation of gifts vary enormously by family, region, and social class. And the custom does not function as a mark of deference or respect towards a person or sacred object; it is an expression of affection, a discharge of obligation, a participation in a shared seasonal rhythm. The definition, drawn from customs of formal deference, mistook the features of that narrow class for the features of custom as such.

background_cases: The Royal Navy, with its Admiralty, its ranks, its regulations, and its dockyards, is an institution that has shaped British life for centuries.
The Inns of Court, with their benchers, their barristers, their dining customs, and their ancient halls, constitute an institution governing the practice of law in England.
The College of Heralds, with its kings of arms, its pursuivants, its records of armorial bearings, and its procedures for granting arms, is an institution regulating matters of heraldic status.
definition: An arrangement A constitutes a social institution if and only if (i) A serves a single, clearly defined social function, (ii) A is governed by a body of rules that have been formally articulated and are available for inspection, and (iii) A contains within itself mechanisms for adjudicating disputes among its members.
conditions: A serves a single, clearly defined social function
A is governed by a body of rules that have been formally articulated and are available for inspection
A contains within itself mechanisms for adjudicating disputes among its members
counterexample: The family is an institution, yet it does not serve a single, clearly defined social function. It serves many: the rearing of children, the regulation of sexual conduct, the organisation of domestic economy, the provision of emotional sustenance, the transmission of property, and the care of the aged. Its governing rules are not formally articulated or available for inspection; the obligations of family members are sustained by custom, sentiment, and tacit understanding rather than by written codes. And it does not contain within itself formal mechanisms for adjudicating disputes; family quarrels are settled — or not settled — by negotiation, compromise, the authority of senior members, or the sheer force of affection and habit.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all formal, single-purpose institutions — a military organisation, a legal society, a heraldic college — each serving a defined function, governed by articulated rules, and possessing internal mechanisms for dispute resolution. The definition abstracted these features as essential to institutional existence. But the cases were drawn from the domain of specialised, formally organised bodies, and the definition mistook their features for the features of institutions as such. The family reveals that an institution may serve multiple, overlapping, and ill-defined functions; may be governed by unarticulated customs and sentiments rather than by formal rules; and may settle its internal disputes through informal means rather than through any structured mechanism. The definition confused the properties of bureaucratic, single-purpose institutions with the necessary conditions of institutional life.
passage:
Consider three arrangements that are unquestionably social institutions. The Royal Navy, with its Admiralty, its hierarchy of ranks from admiral to ordinary seaman, its detailed regulations governing every aspect of naval life, and its dockyards stretching from Portsmouth to Devonport. The Inns of Court, with their benchers and barristers, their elaborate dining customs, their moots and readings, and their ancient halls in which the common law has been studied for centuries. The College of Heralds, with its kings of arms and pursuivants, its meticulously maintained records of armorial bearings, and its formal procedures for the granting and marshalling of coats of arms. From these cases one might extract a definition: an arrangement constitutes a social institution if and only if it serves a single, clearly defined social function, is governed by formally articulated rules available for inspection, and contains within itself mechanisms for adjudicating disputes among its members. The background cases satisfy all three conditions.

But one must consider the family. The family is an institution — one of the oldest and most fundamental institutions of human social life. It has persisted, in various forms, across every known society. Yet it does not serve a single, clearly defined social function. It serves many functions simultaneously, and the boundaries between them are blurred: the rearing and socialisation of children, the regulation of sexual conduct, the organisation of domestic economy, the provision of emotional sustenance, the transmission of property across generations, the care of the aged and infirm. These functions overlap, conflict, and resist neat separation. The family is not governed by formally articulated rules available for inspection. Its obligations — the duties of parent to child, of spouse to spouse, of sibling to sibling — are sustained by custom, sentiment, religious precept, and tacit understanding rather than by any written code. One searches in vain for the rulebook of the family. And the family does not contain within itself formal mechanisms for adjudicating disputes. Family quarrels are settled, when they are settled at all, through negotiation, compromise, the quiet authority of a respected elder, or the sheer force of affection and long habit. The definition, drawn from formally organised, single-purpose institutions, mistook their bureaucratic features for the essence of institutional life, and thereby failed to accommodate the most ancient institution of all.

background_cases: French culture in the age of Louis XIV, unified under the authority of the Académie française, the court at Versailles, and a single national language, presenting a coherent and harmonious cultural whole.
Japanese culture during the Edo period, unified under the shogunate's control, with a single official language, a uniform system of social ranks, and a tightly regulated cultural life.
Athenian culture in the age of Pericles, unified by a common language, common religious festivals, common political institutions, and a shared body of myth and literature.
definition: A society S possesses a genuine culture if and only if (i) the cultural life of S is unified and internally consistent, without significant contradiction or conflict among its elements, (ii) the cultural life of S is directed and shaped by a central authority or a dominant class, and (iii) the members of S experience their culture as a harmonious whole.
conditions: the cultural life of S is unified and internally consistent, without significant contradiction or conflict among its elements
the cultural life of S is directed and shaped by a central authority or a dominant class
the members of S experience their culture as a harmonious whole
counterexample: The culture of mediaeval Christendom was riven with contradiction and internal conflict. The ideals of the Church — poverty, chastity, humility — stood in perpetual tension with the ideals of the feudal aristocracy — martial prowess, honour, courtly love. No single authority could resolve this tension; the Pope and the Emperor contended for supremacy, and neither fully controlled the cultural life of the whole. The members of the society did not experience their culture as a harmonious whole; they experienced it as a field of competing claims and loyalties. Yet mediaeval Christendom possessed a culture of extraordinary richness and depth.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all societies in which cultural life appeared unified, centrally directed, and experienced as harmonious — Bourbon France, Edo Japan, Periclean Athens. The definition abstracted unity, central direction, and experienced harmony as essential to genuine culture. But these were features of cultures viewed from above, at a level of abstraction that concealed their internal tensions. The case of mediaeval Christendom reveals that a culture may be riven with contradiction — between the ideals of the Church and those of the aristocracy, between the claims of the papacy and those of secular power — yet remain a genuine and immensely productive culture. The definition mistook the appearance of unity, achievable only in certain highly centralised societies, for a necessary condition of cultural existence, when in truth culture may thrive upon its own internal contradictions.
passage:
Consider three societies recognised as possessing genuine cultures. France under Louis XIV, where the Académie française regulated the language, the court at Versailles set the standard for manners and taste, and cultural life was unified under the direction of a powerful centralising monarchy. Japan during the Edo period, where the shogunate maintained a tightly regulated cultural order, a single official language, a uniform system of social ranks, and a controlled, internally consistent cultural life. Athenian culture in the fifth century before Christ, unified by the Greek language, by common religious festivals, by the shared institutions of the polis, and by a body of myth and literature known to all citizens. From these cases one might extract a definition: a society possesses a genuine culture if and only if its cultural life is unified and internally consistent, is directed and shaped by a central authority or a dominant class, and is experienced by its members as a harmonious whole.

But one must consider mediaeval Christendom. The culture of Western Europe in the twelfth and thirteenth centuries was a culture of extraordinary richness — a culture that produced the Gothic cathedral, the scholastic philosophy of Aquinas, the poetry of Dante, the troubadour lyric, and the institutions of the university. Yet it was anything but unified and internally consistent. The ideals of the Church — poverty, chastity, obedience, humility before God — stood in perpetual and unresolved tension with the ideals of the feudal aristocracy — martial valour, personal honour, loyalty to one's lord, and the elaborate courtly rituals of love. The monastic ideal and the chivalric ideal coexisted, but they did not cohere; they pulled in opposite directions, and the tension between them generated much of the creative energy of the period. No single authority directed the cultural life of the whole. The Pope claimed universal spiritual authority; the Emperor claimed universal temporal authority; neither prevailed, and the contest between them shaped the political and cultural history of centuries. The members of the society did not experience their culture as a harmonious whole; they experienced it as a field of competing claims, rival loyalties, and irreconcilable ideals. The definition, drawn from cases of apparent cultural unity, mistook the surface harmony of centrally directed societies for a necessary condition of culture, when in truth a culture may be animated and enriched by its own contradictions.

background_cases: The norm against public nudity, observed in virtually all settled societies, prohibiting the exposure of the unclothed body in public places.
The norm against eating human flesh, observed in virtually all known societies, prohibiting the consumption of the dead.
The norm against incest, observed in virtually all human societies, prohibiting sexual relations between close kin.
definition: A rule R constitutes a fundamental social norm if and only if (i) R is observed in all known human societies without exception, (ii) R admits of no legitimate exceptions within any society that observes it, and (iii) violation of R provokes a reaction of intense moral disgust in all members of the society.
conditions: R is observed in all known human societies without exception
R admits of no legitimate exceptions within any society that observes it
violation of R provokes a reaction of intense moral disgust in all members of the society
counterexample: The norm of reciprocity — that one ought to return a benefit received — is a fundamental social norm. It is observed, in some form, in all known human societies. Yet it admits of legitimate exceptions: one is not obliged to reciprocate a gift given with malicious intent, and certain relationships — such as that of parent to young child — are understood to be asymmetric, with no expectation of return. And violation of the norm does not provoke intense moral disgust; the person who fails to return a favour is regarded as ungrateful or discourteous, but the reaction is one of disapproval rather than disgust. Yet reciprocity is among the most fundamental norms of social life.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all prohibitive norms characterised by universality, exceptionlessness, and the capacity to provoke intense disgust — norms against nudity, cannibalism, and incest. The definition abstracted these features as essential to any fundamental norm. But the cases were drawn from the class of strong taboos, and the definition mistook the features of taboo for the features of fundamental norms in general. The norm of reciprocity reveals that a norm may be fundamental — in the sense of being indispensable to any form of social cooperation — while admitting of legitimate exceptions and provoking, when violated, not disgust but the milder response of disapproval. The definition confused the emotional intensity of taboo violation with the sociological fundamentality of normative requirements.
passage:
Consider three norms that appear to be fundamental to human social life. The norm against public nudity, observed in virtually all settled societies, which prohibits the exposure of the unclothed body in public. The norm against the consumption of human flesh, observed in virtually all known societies, the violation of which provokes the deepest revulsion. The norm against incest, observed in all human societies, which prohibits sexual relations between close kin. From these cases one might extract a definition: a rule constitutes a fundamental social norm if and only if it is observed in all known human societies without exception, it admits of no legitimate exceptions within any society that observes it, and its violation provokes a reaction of intense moral disgust in all members of the society. The three cases satisfy all three conditions, and the definition appears to identify the marks of a truly fundamental norm.

But one must consider the norm of reciprocity. The obligation to return a benefit received — to repay a gift, to return a favour, to discharge a debt of gratitude — is a norm observed, in some form, in every known human society. Anthropologists have documented its presence in cultures as diverse as those of Melanesia, West Africa, pre-modern Japan, and the villages of rural Europe. It is, by any reasonable reckoning, a fundamental norm — indeed, some scholars have argued that it is the most fundamental of all social norms, the very foundation upon which cooperation and exchange are built. Yet it admits of legitimate exceptions. One is not obliged to reciprocate a gift given with manipulative or malicious intent. Certain relationships are understood to be inherently asymmetric: the parent gives to the young child without expectation of return, and no one regards this asymmetry as a failure of reciprocity. And violation of the norm does not provoke intense moral disgust. The person who fails to return a favour, who neglects to reciprocate a kindness, is regarded as ungrateful, discourteous, perhaps meanly selfish — but the reaction is one of social disapproval, not of the visceral revulsion that attends violations of the taboos against cannibalism or incest. The definition, drawn from cases of strong taboo, mistook the emotional intensity of taboo violation for the essential mark of normative fundamentality, when in truth a norm may be fundamental to the structure of social life without provoking, when violated, any reaction stronger than disapproval and mild contempt.

background_cases: Two neighbouring farmers cooperate to build a fence along their shared boundary, each contributing labour and materials, and both benefiting from the enclosure of their respective fields.
Members of a fire brigade cooperate to extinguish a blaze, each performing an assigned role — manning the pump, directing the hose, carrying the ladder — towards the shared end of saving a building.
The crew of a sailing vessel cooperates to navigate a storm, each sailor attending to his station, trimming the sails, working the bilge pump, and following the captain's orders.
definition: An interaction I constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) the parties to I are engaged in a joint physical activity requiring coordinated bodily effort, (ii) the parties are co-present in the same physical space during the interaction, and (iii) the interaction has a definite temporal endpoint at which the cooperative task is completed.
conditions: the parties to I are engaged in a joint physical activity requiring coordinated bodily effort
the parties are co-present in the same physical space during the interaction
the interaction has a definite temporal endpoint at which the cooperative task is completed
counterexample: Consider the cooperation involved in the compilation of a great dictionary — the Oxford English Dictionary, let us say. Hundreds of volunteer readers, scattered across Britain and the English-speaking world, cooperated with the editors over a period of decades. Each reader selected passages from books, copied out quotations illustrating the use of words, and posted them by mail to the editors in Oxford. The readers were not engaged in joint physical activity; they sat alone in their studies, reading and writing. They were not co-present in the same space; they were dispersed across continents. And the task had no definite temporal endpoint; it continued for decades and was, in a sense, never completed. Yet the dictionary was a cooperative achievement of the first order.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of physical, co-located, temporally bounded cooperation — building a fence, fighting a fire, sailing through a storm. The definition abstracted joint physical activity, co-presence, and temporal boundedness as essential to cooperation. But the cases were drawn from a narrow class of immediately practical, bodily cooperative tasks. The dictionary project reveals that cooperation may be intellectual rather than physical, dispersed rather than co-located, and indefinitely extended rather than temporally bounded. The definition confused the features of one familiar form of cooperation — the joint physical task performed by persons working side by side — with the features of cooperation as such.
passage:
Consider three interactions that are plainly cooperative. Two farmers building a fence along their shared boundary, each contributing labour and materials, working side by side in the same field. Members of a fire brigade fighting a blaze, each performing an assigned role — one manning the pump, another directing the hose, a third carrying the ladder — towards the shared end of saving a building. The crew of a sailing vessel navigating a storm, each sailor at his station, trimming sails and working the bilge pump under the captain's direction. From these cases one might extract a definition: an interaction constitutes cooperation if and only if the parties are engaged in a joint physical activity requiring coordinated bodily effort, are co-present in the same physical space, and the interaction has a definite temporal endpoint at which the task is completed. The background cases all satisfy these conditions.

But one must consider the compilation of a great dictionary. The Oxford English Dictionary was, in its inception, a cooperative enterprise of extraordinary scope. Hundreds of volunteer readers, scattered across Britain, the colonies, and the United States, cooperated with the editorial staff in Oxford over a period spanning decades. Each reader undertook to read through certain books, to identify passages illustrating the use of particular words, to copy out the quotations on slips of paper, and to post these slips to the editors. The editors, in turn, sorted the slips, selected the illustrative quotations, and composed the dictionary entries. The interaction was cooperative in the fullest sense — the dictionary could not have been produced without the joint labour of readers and editors, and each party's contribution was essential to the finished work. Yet the readers were not engaged in joint physical activity requiring coordinated bodily effort; each sat alone in a study, reading and writing, with no physical coordination required between one reader and another. The readers were not co-present in the same space; they were dispersed across continents, connected to the editors only by the postal service. And the task had no definite temporal endpoint; it continued for decades, and the first edition was not completed until seventy years after the project's inception. The definition, drawn from cases of immediate, physical, co-located cooperation, mistook the features of that particular form for the features of cooperation itself.

background_cases: A farmer trusts his neighbour to look after his cattle whilst he is away at market, having seen his neighbour care for animals competently on many previous occasions.
A master craftsman trusts his journeyman to complete a commission in his absence, having trained the journeyman himself and observed his skill over several years.
A merchant trusts his factor in a distant port to sell goods honestly and remit the proceeds, having received accurate accounts from the factor over many trading seasons.
definition: A person S trusts a person T with respect to a task X if and only if (i) S has direct, first-hand experience of T performing X or tasks relevantly similar to X, (ii) S's expectation of T's competent and honest performance is proportional to the amount of evidence S has accumulated, and (iii) S can withdraw trust incrementally as counter-evidence accumulates.
conditions: S has direct, first-hand experience of T performing X or tasks relevantly similar to X
S's expectation of T's competent and honest performance is proportional to the amount of evidence S has accumulated
S can withdraw trust incrementally as counter-evidence accumulates
counterexample: Consider the trust that the members of a besieged garrison place in a messenger who volunteers to slip through the enemy lines and carry a plea for reinforcement to a distant allied force. The garrison has no direct experience of this messenger performing such a task; the circumstances are unprecedented. The trust they place in him is not proportional to accumulated evidence — they have none — but is an act of desperate confidence in his courage and loyalty. And they cannot withdraw trust incrementally; once the messenger has departed, they have committed everything to his success. They trust him wholly or not at all. Yet their trust is genuine and consequential.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of trust built up gradually through repeated, evidence-based experience — the farmer who has seen his neighbour tend cattle, the master who has trained his journeyman, the merchant who has received honest accounts. The definition abstracted direct experience, proportional expectation, and incremental withdrawal as essential features of trust. But the cases were drawn from stable, ongoing relationships in which trust could develop gradually and be adjusted over time. The besieged garrison reveals that trust may be extended in a single, unrepeatable act, without prior evidence, and without the possibility of gradual withdrawal. The definition confused the features of evidence-based, incrementally adjustable trust with the features of trust as such, overlooking the possibility of trust as a radical, all-or-nothing commitment made under conditions of extreme uncertainty.
passage:
Consider three cases of trust. A farmer trusts his neighbour to look after his cattle whilst he is away at market; he has seen the neighbour tend animals competently on many previous occasions, and his trust has grown gradually with each satisfactory experience. A master craftsman trusts his journeyman to complete a commission; the master trained the journeyman himself, observed his work over several years, and has accumulated extensive evidence of the journeyman's skill and reliability. A merchant trusts his factor in a distant port; the factor has rendered honest accounts over many trading seasons, and the merchant's confidence in him is proportional to the body of evidence accumulated. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person trusts another with respect to a task if and only if the first person has direct experience of the second performing the relevant task, the expectation of competent and honest performance is proportional to accumulated evidence, and trust can be withdrawn incrementally as counter-evidence emerges.

But one must consider the besieged garrison. A city is surrounded by an enemy army, and its defenders face destruction unless reinforcements arrive from a distant ally. A man — a soldier, perhaps, or a civilian of known courage — volunteers to slip through the enemy lines under cover of darkness, carrying a message to the allied commander. The garrison has no direct experience of this man performing such a task; the circumstances are unprecedented, and no one in the garrison has ever attempted anything similar. The trust they place in him is not proportional to accumulated evidence; they have no evidence at all, only a rapid assessment of his character and a desperate need. And they cannot withdraw trust incrementally; once the messenger has left, they have staked everything upon his success. They cannot recall him, cannot adjust the degree of their reliance, cannot hedge their commitment. They trust him wholly or not at all. Yet the trust is genuine — as genuine as any trust can be — and upon it may depend the survival of the entire garrison. The definition, drawn from cases of gradually accumulated, evidence-based trust, mistook the features of that common and important form of trust for the features of trust itself, and thereby excluded the radical, all-or-nothing trust that is extended in moments of crisis, without evidence, and without the possibility of withdrawal.

background_cases: The citizens of a Swiss canton assembling in a Landsgemeinde to vote, by show of hands, on the laws that will govern them, each citizen participating directly in the legislative act.
The freemen of an Icelandic commonwealth gathering at the Althing to adjudicate disputes and establish laws by collective deliberation, each freeman possessing an equal voice.
The members of a New England town meeting, assembling in the meeting house to decide, by majority vote, on the taxation, expenditure, and regulations of the town.
definition: A political arrangement A rests upon a genuine social contract if and only if (i) every member of the society has participated directly in the formulation of the terms of A, (ii) the terms of A are periodically reaffirmed through acts of direct collective decision-making, and (iii) any member who disagrees with the terms may freely withdraw from the arrangement without penalty.
conditions: every member of the society has participated directly in the formulation of the terms of A
the terms of A are periodically reaffirmed through acts of direct collective decision-making
any member who disagrees with the terms may freely withdraw from the arrangement without penalty
counterexample: The political arrangements of modern Britain rest upon a social contract — an implicit understanding between the state and the citizenry regarding the terms of political authority, the rights of the individual, and the obligations of government.
