Yet not every member of the society has participated directly in formulating these terms; the vast majority have inherited them. The terms are not periodically reaffirmed through acts of direct collective decision-making; they are maintained through representative institutions, judicial interpretation, and the slow evolution of convention. And a citizen who disagrees with the terms cannot freely withdraw without penalty; departure from the political community entails the abandonment of property, livelihood, and social ties. Yet the arrangement rests upon a social contract in a perfectly meaningful sense.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all small-scale polities practising direct democracy — the Swiss canton, the Icelandic commonwealth, the New England town meeting — in which every citizen participated directly, terms were periodically reaffirmed through collective assembly, and the scale of the community made withdrawal at least conceivable. The definition abstracted direct participation, periodic reaffirmation, and free withdrawal as essential to any genuine social contract. But the cases were drawn from a narrow class of small, direct democracies, and the definition mistook the features of these particular arrangements for the features of the social contract as such. The case of modern Britain reveals that a social contract may operate on a vast scale through representative institutions, may be sustained by tacit acquiescence and gradual evolution rather than by periodic reaffirmation, and may bind its members through ties so deep that withdrawal, though theoretically possible, is not a realistic option. The definition confused the conditions of a small-scale, participatory social contract with the necessary conditions of any social contract whatsoever.
passage:
Consider three political arrangements that rest upon something very like a social contract. The citizens of a Swiss canton, assembling in a Landsgemeinde — the open-air assembly — to vote by show of hands on the laws that will govern them, each citizen participating directly in the legislative act. The freemen of the Icelandic commonwealth, gathering at the Althing each summer to adjudicate disputes and establish laws by collective deliberation, each freeman possessing an equal voice. The members of a New England town meeting, assembling in the meeting house to decide, by majority vote, on matters of local taxation, expenditure, and regulation. From these cases one might extract a definition: a political arrangement rests upon a genuine social contract if and only if every member of the society has participated directly in formulating its terms, the terms are periodically reaffirmed through acts of direct collective decision-making, and any member who disagrees may freely withdraw without penalty.

But one must consider modern Britain. The political arrangements of the United Kingdom rest upon a social contract in a perfectly meaningful sense — an implicit understanding between the state and the citizenry regarding the terms upon which political authority is exercised, the rights that individuals may claim against the state, and the obligations that the state owes to its members. This understanding has been shaped by centuries of struggle, negotiation, and accommodation — by Magna Carta, by the Civil War, by the Glorious Revolution, by the Reform Acts, and by innumerable smaller adjustments. Yet not every member of the society has participated directly in formulating its terms; the overwhelming majority have inherited an arrangement that was shaped by their predecessors. The terms are not periodically reaffirmed through acts of direct collective decision-making; they are maintained through representative institutions — Parliament, the courts, the civil service — and through the slow, largely unnoticed evolution of convention and practice. And a citizen who disagrees with the terms cannot freely withdraw without penalty; to leave the political community is to abandon one's property, one's livelihood, one's family, and the entire web of social ties that constitute one's life. Withdrawal is theoretically possible but practically ruinous. The definition, drawn from small-scale polities practising direct democracy, mistook the features of those arrangements — direct participation, periodic reaffirmation, feasible withdrawal — for the necessary conditions of any social contract, when in truth the social contract of a large, complex, representative democracy operates through quite different mechanisms.

background_cases: A man lends money to his neighbour, trusting that the neighbour will repay the loan; the trust is based on personal acquaintance and a history of reliable dealings between them.
A merchant extends credit to a customer with whom he has traded for many years, trusting in the customer's good character and past punctuality of payment.
A farmer leaves his tools in an unlocked shed, trusting that his neighbours, whom he has known since childhood, will not take them.
definition: A person P trusts a person Q if and only if (i) P and Q are personally acquainted — that is, P has direct knowledge of Q's character gained through repeated personal interaction, (ii) P has observed Q acting reliably in the past and infers that Q will act reliably in the future, and (iii) the trust is reciprocal — Q also trusts P.
conditions: P and Q are personally acquainted through repeated interaction
P has observed Q acting reliably in the past
the trust is reciprocal — Q also trusts P
counterexample: Consider the trust that a passenger places in the pilot of an aeroplane. The passenger boards the aircraft, entrusts his life to the pilot, and sits calmly throughout the flight in the confidence that the pilot will convey him safely to his destination. Yet the passenger is not personally acquainted with the pilot; he has never met the pilot, does not know the pilot's name, and has no direct knowledge of the pilot's character. He has not observed the pilot acting reliably in the past; he has never seen the pilot fly. And the trust is not reciprocal; the pilot does not trust the passenger in any comparable sense — the pilot does not entrust his safety to the passenger. All three conditions fail, yet the passenger trusts the pilot.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the neighbour, the customer, the fellow villager — were all instances of trust grounded in personal acquaintance, direct observation, and reciprocal relations within a small community. The definition abstracted personal knowledge, past observation, and reciprocity as essential to trust. But the background cases were drawn from the world of face-to-face community, where trust is between persons who know each other, and the definition mistook the features of that form of trust for the features of trust as such. The aeroplane passenger reveals that trust may be placed in strangers, may rest upon institutional guarantees rather than personal acquaintance, and may be wholly one-directional. The definition confused the trust of the village with the trust of modern social life, in which we daily entrust our lives and welfare to persons we have never met, on the strength of institutional structures — licensing, training, regulation — rather than personal knowledge.
passage:
Consider three cases in which trust is plainly present. A man lends money to his neighbour, trusting that the loan will be repaid. His trust is grounded in personal acquaintance; he has known the neighbour for twenty years, has observed him in many dealings, and has found him always to be punctual in his obligations. A merchant extends credit to a long-standing customer, trusting in the customer's good character, which he has had ample opportunity to observe over many years of trade. A farmer leaves his tools in an unlocked shed, trusting that his neighbours — men he has known since childhood — will respect his property. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person trusts another if and only if the two are personally acquainted through repeated interaction, the first has observed the second acting reliably in the past, and the trust is reciprocal.

But one must consider the trust that a passenger places in the pilot of an aeroplane. The passenger arrives at the airport, presents his ticket, walks through the boarding gate, takes his seat, fastens his belt, and entrusts his life — without reservation and without anxiety — to the person who will fly the aircraft. Yet the passenger has never met the pilot. He does not know the pilot's name, has no acquaintance with the pilot's character, and has never observed the pilot performing any action, reliable or otherwise. His trust is not grounded in personal knowledge or past observation; it is grounded in something altogether different — in the institutional framework of civil aviation, in the knowledge that the pilot has been trained, examined, and licensed by a regulatory authority, that the aircraft has been inspected and certified, and that the airline is subject to safety standards enforced by law. The trust is not reciprocal in any meaningful sense; the pilot does not entrust his safety to the passenger, and the relation between them is entirely asymmetrical. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet the trust is genuine and profound — as profound as any trust between neighbours, for it is a trust in which one's very life is at stake. The background cases were drawn from the world of face-to-face community, where trust is personal, observational, and reciprocal, and the definition abstracted these features as essential. The aeroplane reveals that the trust which sustains modern social life is largely impersonal, institutional, and unilateral, resting not upon the direct knowledge of another's character but upon the reliability of systems, certifications, and regulatory structures that the person who trusts may never have examined and could not evaluate.

background_cases: A farming village in the English countryside constitutes a community: the villagers live near one another, they know one another by name, they gather at the church, the public house, and the village fête, and they share a common way of life rooted in the agricultural cycle.

A neighbourhood in a small town constitutes a community: the residents live on adjacent streets, their children attend the same school, they meet at the shops and the park, and they share an understanding of local customs and expectations.
A monastic order constitutes a community: the monks live together in a single building, share meals, worship together, and are bound by a common rule of life.
definition: A group of persons G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G live in close physical proximity to one another, (ii) the members of G interact face to face on a frequent basis, and (iii) the members of G are bound together by ties of personal affection or friendship.
conditions: the members live in close physical proximity
the members interact face to face frequently
the members are bound by ties of personal affection or friendship
counterexample: Consider the community of Orthodox Jews scattered across many cities and countries. They do not all live in close physical proximity; they are found in New York, London, Jerusalem, Melbourne, and many other places. They do not all interact face to face on a frequent basis; a member in London may never meet a member in Melbourne. And they are not all bound by ties of personal affection; most of them are strangers to one another. Yet they constitute a community in a deep and meaningful sense. They share a body of religious law, a calendar of observances, a set of dietary practices, a tradition of textual study, and a sense of mutual obligation that transcends geography. A member of the community who arrives in a strange city can rely upon the hospitality and assistance of fellow members whom he has never met. The bonds that unite them are not proximity, frequent contact, or personal affection, but shared practice, shared law, and shared identity.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the village, the neighbourhood, the monastery — were all communities of place, in which the members live near one another and interact face to face. The definition abstracted proximity, frequent interaction, and personal affection as essential to community. But the background cases were drawn from the narrow domain of geographically concentrated groups, and the definition mistook the conditions of local community for the conditions of community as such. The dispersed religious community reveals that a community may be constituted by shared practice, shared law, and shared identity, without proximity, frequent face-to-face contact, or ties of personal affection among all its members.
passage:
Consider three groups that are paradigmatically communities. A farming village in the English countryside is a community: the villagers live near one another, know one another by name, and gather regularly at the church, the public house, and the annual fête. They share a way of life rooted in the agricultural calendar — the rhythms of planting and harvest, the common concern with weather and soil. A neighbourhood in a small town is a community: the residents live on adjacent streets, their children play together and attend the same school, and they meet at the corner shop and the park. They share a familiarity with one another's habits and circumstances that comes of living at close quarters. A monastic order is a community: the monks live together under a single roof, share meals and worship, and are bound by a common rule of life and by the personal bonds that form among men who live in such intimacy. From these cases one might extract a definition: a group constitutes a community if and only if the members live in close physical proximity, interact face to face frequently, and are bound by ties of personal affection or friendship.

But one must consider the community of Orthodox Jews dispersed across many countries. Members of this community live in New York, London, Jerusalem, Antwerp, Melbourne, and scores of other cities. They are separated by thousands of miles; most of them will never meet one another. They do not interact face to face on a frequent basis; a family in London may have no face-to-face contact with a family in Buenos Aires from one decade to the next. And they are not all bound by ties of personal affection; most of them are strangers to one another in the ordinary sense of the term. Yet they constitute a community of extraordinary cohesion and vitality. They share a body of religious law that governs every aspect of daily life — what they eat, how they dress, when they work and when they rest. They share a calendar of festivals and fasts, a tradition of textual study stretching back thousands of years, and a system of education that transmits the tradition to each new generation. A member of the community who finds himself in a strange city has only to locate a synagogue to be welcomed, fed, and assisted by fellow members whom he has never met and who have never heard his name. The obligations of mutual aid and hospitality are not contingent upon personal acquaintance; they flow from membership in the community itself. The definition, drawn from the experience of villages and neighbourhoods, required proximity, frequent face-to-face contact, and personal affection. The dispersed religious community reveals that community may be constituted by shared practice, shared law, and shared identity — bonds that hold across vast distances and among persons who have never met — and that the definition mistook the conditions of local community for the conditions of community as such.

background_cases: Two neighbours agree to help one another with a project: one holds the ladder whilst the other climbs to repair the roof. Each knows the other's role, and both work towards the shared goal of completing the repair.
A string quartet rehearses a piece of chamber music: each player knows his part, listens to the others, and adjusts his playing so that the four parts blend into a unified performance.

A team of scientists collaborates on a research paper: each contributes data or analysis, and they coordinate their efforts by discussion and division of labour towards the shared goal of completing the investigation.
definition: Two or more persons cooperate if and only if (i) each person knows the shared goal and their or their role in achieving it, (ii) each person voluntarily chooses to participate, and (iii) the persons interact directly during the cooperative activity — that is, each person's actions are visibly responsive to the actions of the others.
conditions: each person knows the shared goal and their or their role
each person voluntarily participates
the persons interact directly — each is visibly responsive to the others
counterexample: Consider the cooperation of blood donors. Thousands of persons in a city each go to a clinic, at different times and different locations, and donate blood. They share a goal — the maintenance of the blood supply for the hospitals of the city. Each volunteers freely. Yet they do not interact directly with one another during the cooperative activity. The donor who gives blood on Monday morning in one clinic is not visibly responsive to the donor who gives blood on Wednesday afternoon in another. They never meet; they never see one another's actions; they never adjust their behaviour in response to what the others are doing. Their cooperation is mediated entirely by the institution of the blood bank, which collects, stores, and distributes the donations. The third condition fails, yet the donors cooperate — they jointly maintain the blood supply through their individual, uncoordinated contributions.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the neighbours, the quartet, the scientists — were all instances of direct, face-to-face cooperation, in which each participant could see and respond to the others' actions. The definition abstracted direct interaction as essential to cooperation. But the background cases were drawn from the domain of small-scale, collocated collaboration, and the definition mistook the directness of those interactions for a necessary condition of cooperation as such. The blood donors reveal that cooperation may be mediated by institutions, distributed across time and space, and accomplished by persons who never meet or interact, yet who jointly achieve a shared goal through their individual contributions.
passage:
Consider three cases of cooperation. Two neighbours agree to repair a roof: one holds the ladder whilst the other climbs up and replaces the damaged tiles. Each knows the shared goal — a sound roof — and each is visibly responsive to the other: the man on the ground steadies the ladder when the man on the roof shifts his weight, and the man on the roof waits for the signal before descending. A string quartet rehearses a work of chamber music: each player knows his part, listens to the others, and adjusts his tempo, his dynamics, and his phrasing so that the four parts blend into a unified performance. A team of scientists collaborates on a research paper: they meet, discuss hypotheses, divide the analysis, and integrate their contributions into a single work. In each case, the participants know the goal and their roles, they participate voluntarily, and they interact directly — each person's actions are visibly responsive to those of the others. From these cases one might extract a definition: persons cooperate if and only if each knows the shared goal and his or her role, each participates voluntarily, and the persons interact directly with one another.

But one must consider the cooperation of blood donors in a large city. Thousands of individuals, over the course of a year, visit clinics at different times and in different locations and donate blood. Each donor shares a goal — the maintenance of the city's blood supply, so that hospitals may have blood available when it is needed. Each volunteers freely; no one is compelled to give blood. Yet the donors do not interact directly with one another. The woman who donates blood on a Monday morning in a clinic in the east of the city never meets the man who donates on a Friday afternoon in a clinic in the west. She does not see his action; he does not see hers. Neither adjusts his or her behaviour in response to the other. They are strangers, separated in time and space, and their contributions are independent of one another. Their cooperation is mediated entirely by the institution of the blood bank, which collects the individual donations, tests them, stores them, and distributes them to the hospitals. The donors jointly maintain the blood supply, but they do so without any direct interaction. The third condition of the definition fails. The background cases were all instances of small-scale, collocated cooperation — two neighbours, four musicians, a team of scientists — in which the participants could see and respond to one another. The blood donors reveal that cooperation may be distributed across time and space, mediated by institutions, and accomplished by persons who never meet, and that the definition confused the directness of small-group collaboration with a necessary condition of cooperation itself.

background_cases: The tradition of common law in England has been maintained for centuries: judges follow precedents established by their predecessors, and the body of law grows by the gradual accumulation of decisions, each building upon what came before.

The tradition of classical music performance has been maintained across generations: each generation of musicians learns the repertoire from the previous generation, and the conventions of performance — tempo, dynamics, ornamentation — are transmitted from teacher to pupil.

The tradition of parliamentary debate in the House of Commons has been maintained since the seventeenth century: new members learn the procedures, the forms of address, and the conventions of debate from experienced members, and the customs are upheld by the Speaker and the clerks.
definition: A practice P is a tradition if and only if (i) P is transmitted from one generation to the next through a recognised chain of practitioners — each generation learns from the previous one, (ii) P has an identifiable point of origin — a moment, a founding figure, or an inaugurating event from which the practice descends, and (iii) the practitioners of P are conscious of participating in a tradition — they understand themselves as continuing what their predecessors began.
conditions: P is transmitted through a recognised chain of practitioners
P has an identifiable point of origin
the practitioners are conscious of participating in a tradition
counterexample: Consider the tradition of hospitality to strangers as it is practised in many rural societies. In such communities, a traveller who arrives at a household is fed, sheltered, and treated as an honoured guest, often at considerable expense and inconvenience to the host. This practice is a tradition — it has been observed for as long as anyone can remember, and it is transmitted from parents to children as one of the deepest obligations of communal life. But it has no identifiable point of origin. No one founded the tradition of hospitality; no inaugurating event established it; no founding figure decreed that strangers should be welcomed. The practice is, as far as anyone can tell, immemorial — it extends back beyond the reach of memory and history, and its origin is lost. The second condition fails. Moreover, the practitioners are not always conscious of participating in a tradition; they simply do what has always been done, without reflecting upon it as a tradition or understanding themselves as continuing something that someone began. The third condition may also fail, yet the practice is a tradition.
abductive_insight: The background cases — common law, classical music, parliamentary debate — were all traditions with identifiable origins and self-conscious practitioners who understood themselves as carrying forward an established practice. The definition abstracted identifiable origin and self-conscious participation as essential to tradition. But the background cases were drawn from the domain of institutional, historically documented traditions, and the definition mistook the features of that domain for the features of tradition as such. The tradition of hospitality reveals that a tradition may have no identifiable point of origin — it may be immemorial, extending back beyond the reach of history — and its practitioners may observe it unreflectively, without any consciousness of participating in a tradition. The definition confused the characteristics of articulate, institutionalised traditions with the conditions of tradition itself.
passage:
Consider three practices that are paradigmatically traditions. The tradition of common law in England has been maintained for centuries: each generation of judges follows the precedents established by its predecessors, and the body of law grows by the gradual accumulation of decisions. The chain of transmission is clear — from judge to judge, from century to century — and the origins of common law, though remote, can be traced to identifiable historical developments: the centralisation of royal justice, the establishment of the courts at Westminster, the early Year Books. The tradition of classical music performance is similarly maintained: each generation of musicians learns from the previous one, the conventions of interpretation are transmitted from teacher to pupil, and the practitioners are acutely conscious of continuing a tradition — they speak of the Viennese tradition, the Russian school, the lineage of their teachers. Parliamentary debate in the House of Commons has been a tradition since the seventeenth century, with identifiable origins in the struggles between Crown and Parliament and a continuous chain of practitioners who are conscious of upholding ancient customs. From these cases one might extract a definition: a practice is a tradition if and only if it is transmitted through a recognised chain of practitioners, it has an identifiable point of origin, and the practitioners are conscious of participating in a tradition.

But one must consider the tradition of hospitality to strangers as it is practised in many rural and pastoral societies. In such communities, a stranger who arrives at a household is welcomed, fed, and given shelter for the night, often at considerable cost to the host family. The practice is ancient, universal within the community, and deeply felt: to refuse hospitality to a stranger would be regarded as a grave moral failing, a violation of one of the most fundamental obligations of social life. The practice is transmitted from parents to children — children observe their parents welcoming strangers and grow up to do the same — and it has been observed for as long as anyone can remember. It is a tradition in the fullest sense. Yet it has no identifiable point of origin. No founding figure established it; no inaugurating event set it in motion. If one asks the elder of such a community when the tradition began, he will say that it has been practised since time immemorial — that his grandfather's grandfather observed it, and his grandfather's grandfather before him, and so on, back beyond the reach of memory and record. The origin is lost; there is nothing to identify. And the practitioners are not always conscious of participating in a tradition. They do not say 'We are continuing the tradition of hospitality'; they say 'We welcome strangers because that is what one does.' The practice is habitual, unreflective, as natural as breathing. The second and third conditions of the definition fail. The background cases were all articulate, historically documented traditions with known origins and self-conscious practitioners, and the definition abstracted those features as essential. The tradition of hospitality reveals that a tradition may be immemorial and unreflective, rooted not in a datable act of foundation or a self-conscious sense of continuity but in the deep, unexamined habits of communal life.

background_cases: A village constitutes a community: its members live in close proximity, interact daily, share a local economy and local concerns, and recognise one another as neighbours with mutual obligations.

A religious congregation constitutes a community: its members gather regularly for worship, share a body of beliefs and rituals, and recognise one another as fellow members with mutual duties of care and support.

A trade guild in a medieval city constituted a community: its members practised the same craft, observed common standards, met regularly, and shared responsibility for the training of apprentices and the quality of their products.
definition: A group of persons G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G interact with one another on a regular and sustained basis, (ii) the members of G share a body of common practices, concerns, or commitments, and (iii) the members of G reside in geographical proximity to one another.
conditions: Members interact regularly and sustainedly
Members share common practices, concerns, or commitments
Members reside in geographical proximity
counterexample: Consider a community of mathematicians specialising in number theory, scattered across universities in a dozen countries. They correspond by letter, they meet at annual conferences, they read and critique one another's proofs, and they share a body of common problems, methods, and standards of rigour. They interact regularly; they share common practices and concerns. But they do not reside in geographical proximity — they are separated by continents. Yet they constitute a community in a perfectly recognisable sense: they speak of 'the number theory community,' they feel mutual obligations of intellectual honesty, and they experience a sense of belonging that is no less real for being geographically dispersed.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the village, the congregation, the guild — all happened to involve members who lived or gathered in the same physical space. The definition abstracted geographical proximity as a necessary condition. But what these cases truly shared was not proximity as such but sustained mutual engagement and a sense of shared identity — features that can be realised through correspondence, travel, and shared intellectual endeavour no less than through daily face-to-face contact. The definition captured the spatial form of community but missed the relational substance.
passage:
When one considers the groups most readily called communities — the village, the religious congregation, the medieval trade guild — a pattern of shared features appears. The members interact regularly. They share common practices and concerns. And they live or gather in close geographical proximity. One might extract from these cases the definition: a group constitutes a community if and only if its members interact regularly, share common practices or commitments, and reside in geographical proximity.

Yet this definition, plausible as it seems, fails to accommodate a form of community that has become increasingly prominent in the modern world but is by no means a modern invention. Consider a group of mathematicians specialising in number theory, distributed across universities in many countries. They correspond regularly by letter, they meet at annual conferences, they read and critique one another's work, and they share a common body of problems, methods, and standards. They interact sustainedly and deeply. They share common commitments. But they are separated by continents and do not reside in proximity. Yet they constitute a community in a perfectly recognisable sense — they speak of 'the number theory community,' they acknowledge mutual obligations of intellectual honesty, and they experience a sense of belonging that is no less genuine for being geographically dispersed. The background cases all involved proximity, and the definition elevated this to a necessary condition. But proximity was, in those cases, merely the mechanism through which interaction was sustained — the means, not the essence. The essence was sustained mutual engagement and shared identity, and these can be achieved by correspondence and periodic gathering no less than by living in the same street. The definition captured the spatial mechanism of the background cases but missed the relational substance that proximity served.

background_cases: The tradition of Christmas carol-singing has been passed from generation to generation: parents teach the carols to their children, who grow up and teach them to their own children, preserving the same melodies and words across centuries.

The tradition of Scottish Highland games has been transmitted across generations: the athletic contests — the caber toss, the stone put — are performed today much as they were performed centuries ago, with the same events, the same implements, and substantially the same rules.

The tradition of Japanese tea ceremony has been handed down from master to student for hundreds of years: the precise movements, the choice of utensils, and the aesthetic principles are faithfully preserved across generations.
definition: A practice P is a tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted from generation to generation over a substantial period of time, (ii) P is performed in substantially the same manner as it was performed by earlier generations, and (iii) P is valued by its practitioners in part because of its antiquity — that is, its age and continuity are regarded as reasons for continuing it.
conditions: P has been transmitted across generations over a substantial period
P is performed in substantially the same manner as by earlier generations
P is valued in part because of its antiquity
counterexample: Consider the tradition of English common law. Common law has been transmitted from generation to generation of jurists for many centuries, and its practitioners value it in part because of its antiquity and continuity. But common law has not been performed in substantially the same manner as by earlier generations: it has been continuously modified, reinterpreted, and adapted. Doctrines that were settled in one century are overturned in the next; principles that once governed are abandoned when social circumstances change; new precedents are established that alter the meaning of old ones. The tradition of common law is a tradition precisely of principled adaptation, not of faithful reproduction. The second condition is violated, yet common law is among the most important and recognisable traditions in the Western world.
abductive_insight: The background cases — carol-singing, Highland games, the tea ceremony — all involved traditions characterised by the faithful reproduction of a fixed form. The definition abstracted this reproductive fidelity as a necessary condition. But it concealed the possibility that a tradition might consist not in the preservation of a fixed practice but in the continuous, principled development of a practice across generations — a tradition of adaptation and reinterpretation rather than of static repetition. What the background cases shared was not fixity of form but continuity of transmission and a sense of connection to the past; the definition captured the form of the background cases but missed the deeper principle.
passage:
The most vivid examples of tradition involve practices that are transmitted across generations in substantially unchanged form. Christmas carols are sung with the same melodies that great-grandparents knew. Highland games are contested with the same events and implements as centuries ago. The Japanese tea ceremony preserves precise movements and aesthetic principles across hundreds of years. From these cases one might extract the definition: a practice is a tradition if and only if it has been transmitted across generations, is performed in substantially the same manner as by earlier generations, and is valued in part because of its antiquity.

Yet this definition, drawn from cases of reproductive fidelity, encounters a difficulty when applied to one of the most important traditions in the Western world. Consider the tradition of English common law. It has been transmitted from generation to generation of jurists for many centuries. Its practitioners value it in part because of its age and continuity — the weight of precedent, the authority of long usage. But common law has not been performed in substantially the same manner as by earlier generations. It has been continuously modified: doctrines that were settled in one century have been overturned in the next; principles that once governed have been abandoned when social circumstances changed; new precedents have reshaped the meaning of old ones. The tradition of common law is a tradition of principled evolution, not of faithful reproduction. The background cases were all traditions of static preservation, and the definition captured their reproductive character. But it concealed the possibility that a tradition might consist in the sustained, principled development of a practice — that the transmission itself might be a transmission not of fixed content but of a living, evolving body of thought and practice. What the background cases shared was continuity of transmission and a sense of connection to the past, not invariance of form; the definition captured the specific form of the background cases but missed the more general principle.

background_cases: Two neighbours cooperate to build a fence along their shared property line: each contributes labour, each understands that the other is doing the same, and the goal — a completed fence — is shared between them.
A group of villagers cooperates to harvest a field before a storm: each person reaps a portion, each is aware that the others are similarly engaged, and the common goal is to save the crop.
Members of a string quartet cooperate to perform a piece of music: each plays her part, each listens to the others and adjusts her playing accordingly, and the shared goal is a unified performance.
definition: A group of agents G engages in cooperation if and only if (i) each member of G performs actions that contribute to a shared goal, (ii) each member of G is aware that the other members are similarly contributing, and (iii) the shared goal could not be achieved by any single member acting alone.
conditions: Each member acts to contribute to a shared goal
Each member is aware the others are similarly contributing
The goal could not be achieved by any single member alone
counterexample: Consider two people who cooperate to carry a light table across a room. Each takes one end, they walk together, and the table is moved. Each contributes to the shared goal; each is aware the other is doing the same. But the goal could easily be achieved by either person acting alone — the table is light enough for one person to carry unaided. They cooperate nonetheless: they divide the effort, coordinate their movements, and accomplish the task together. The third condition is not met — the goal is achievable by a single individual — yet the activity is plainly cooperative.
abductive_insight: The background cases — fence-building, harvesting, quartet performance — all involved tasks that genuinely required multiple participants. The definition abstracted this feature as a necessary condition of cooperation. But what the background cases truly shared was the coordination of effort toward a common purpose, not the impossibility of solitary achievement. Cooperation does not require that the goal be unattainable by a single agent; it requires only that multiple agents coordinate their actions toward a shared objective, whether or not any one of them could have managed alone.
passage:
The paradigmatic cases of cooperation — neighbours building a fence, villagers harvesting a field before a storm, musicians performing a quartet — share several features. Each member of the group acts in a way that contributes to a common goal. Each is aware that the others are doing the same. And the goal — a fence, a harvested field, a musical performance — could not be achieved by any single member acting alone. From these cases, one might extract the definition: a group cooperates if and only if each member acts toward a shared goal, each is aware the others are similarly engaged, and the goal could not be achieved by a single individual.

Yet this definition, abstracted from cases of genuine collective necessity, proves too narrow. Consider two people who cooperate to carry a light table across a room. Each takes one end; they walk in step; the table is moved. Each contributes to the shared goal, and each is aware the other is doing the same. But the table is light — either person could carry it alone without difficulty. The third condition is not satisfied: the goal is perfectly achievable by a single individual. Yet the activity is cooperative in every ordinary sense of the word — the two persons coordinate their actions, divide the labour, and accomplish the task together. The background cases all involved tasks that required multiple participants, and the definition elevated this feature to a necessary condition. But what those cases truly shared was not the impossibility of solitary achievement but the coordination of effort toward a common purpose. Cooperation is a manner of acting together, not a response to individual incapacity; it may be motivated by convenience, companionship, or a preference for shared endeavour as much as by necessity. The definition captured the circumstances of the background cases but missed the essence of cooperation itself.

background_cases: The Royal Navy is an institution: it has a formal organisational structure with ranks, regulations, and chains of command; it was founded by a deliberate act of state; and its officers and ratings perform clearly defined roles within a hierarchical system.

The University of Oxford is an institution: it has written statutes, a formal governance structure of colleges and faculties, and designated officers — the Chancellor, the Vice-Chancellor, the Proctors — who administer its operations. It was founded by deliberate acts of endowment and charter.

A court of law is an institution: it has codified procedures, designated officers (judges, clerks, barristers), and a physical location in which proceedings are conducted. Its authority derives from statute and tradition.
definition: A social arrangement S is an institution if and only if (i) S was established by a deliberate founding act — a charter, a statute, or a formal decision, (ii) S has a hierarchical structure with designated officers who exercise authority, and (iii) S has codified rules governing the conduct of its members.
conditions: S was established by a deliberate founding act
S has a hierarchical structure with designated officers
S has codified rules governing members' conduct
counterexample: Consider the institution of the English language. The English language structures the communicative life of hundreds of millions of people; it imposes rules of grammar, syntax, and usage upon its speakers; and it shapes thought, culture, and social interaction in ways that are as profound and pervasive as any formal organisation. But it was not established by a deliberate founding act — no one decreed that English should exist, and no charter or statute brought it into being. It has no hierarchical structure with designated officers — there is no academy, no president of English, no governing body (unlike, say, the Academie francaise, and even that body's authority is limited). And its rules, though real and powerful, are not codified in any authoritative document; they are embedded in the practices of speakers and evolve through usage rather than through legislation. All three conditions fail. Yet the English language is an institution of the first importance.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the Navy, the University, the court — were all formal organisations, established by deliberate acts and governed by explicit hierarchies and rules. The definition generalised from these to require founding acts, hierarchies, and codified rules. But these features characterise formal organisations, not institutions as such. The English language shows that an institution may arise spontaneously, govern conduct through uncodified norms, and lack any hierarchical structure, while nevertheless imposing stable and powerful patterns of behaviour upon millions of people. The definition has conflated the species (formal organisation) with the genus (institution).
passage:
The paradigmatic institutions — the Navy, the University, the court of law — share a common organisational profile. Each was established by a deliberate founding act; each has a hierarchical structure with designated officers; each is governed by codified rules. From these cases one might extract the definition: a social arrangement is an institution if and only if it was deliberately founded, has a hierarchy with officers, and has codified rules.

Yet this definition, shaped by the model of the formal organisation, proves far too narrow for the concept it purports to capture. Consider the English language. The English language is an institution in the fullest sense: it structures the communicative life of hundreds of millions of speakers, imposes norms of grammar and usage, and shapes the thoughts, categories, and social interactions of all who participate in it. Its influence on the lives of its speakers is at least as great as that of any navy or university. But the English language was not established by a deliberate founding act. No charter brought it into being; no legislative body decreed its existence. It emerged gradually, over centuries, from the interactions of Germanic tribes, Norman conquerors, and subsequent waves of borrowing and innovation. It has no hierarchical structure — no president, no board of directors, no chain of command. And its rules are not codified in any authoritative document; they are embedded in the practices of speakers and evolve through usage, not through formal amendment. All three conditions fail, yet the English language is as much an institution as the Royal Navy. The background cases were all formal organisations, and the definition has mistaken the features of formal organisations for the features of institutions as such. What makes something an institution is not its deliberate founding or its formal hierarchy but its capacity to impose stable, recognised, and consequential patterns of behaviour upon the persons who participate in it — and the English language does this with extraordinary power.

background_cases: The norm against theft is a social norm: the great majority of people refrain from stealing; those who steal are condemned and punished; and the norm serves the social function of protecting property, which in turn enables planning, investment, and cooperation.

The norm of promise-keeping is a social norm: the great majority of people keep their promises; those who break promises are condemned and excluded from future cooperative arrangements; and the norm serves the social function of enabling trust and coordination.

The norm of truth-telling is a social norm: the great majority of people tell the truth in most contexts; those who lie habitually are distrusted and ostracised; and the norm serves the social function of making communication reliable and cooperative activity possible.
definition: A rule R is a social norm if and only if (i) the majority of the members of a group G conform to R, (ii) members of G condemn those who violate R, and (iii) R serves an identifiable social function — that is, conformity to R produces a benefit for the group as a whole.
conditions: The majority of G conforms to R
Members of G condemn violators
R serves an identifiable social function
counterexample: Consider the norm, once widespread in many Western societies, requiring women to wear hats in church. The majority of women conformed to this norm. Those who violated it were condemned — subjected to disapproving glances, whispered criticism, and social exclusion. But it is far from clear that the norm served any identifiable social function — that is, that conformity to it produced any benefit for the group as a whole. The requirement of hat-wearing did not protect anyone's interests, did not enable coordination or cooperation, and did not contribute to any identifiable collective good. It was a norm of convention, maintained by the sheer weight of custom and the force of social disapproval. All conditions but the third are met.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the norms against theft, against promise-breaking, against lying — all happened to serve clear social functions, and the definition generalised from this to require that every social norm be functionally beneficial. But the hat-wearing norm reveals that social norms may persist through custom and social pressure alone, without serving any identifiable function. The background cases were not merely norms but useful norms — norms whose social function made their persistence intelligible. The definition has confused a feature that explains why certain norms arise and persist with a necessary condition of being a norm at all.
passage:
The norms most often discussed in social philosophy — the norms against theft, against promise-breaking, against lying — share a common profile. The majority conform; violators are condemned; and the norm serves a clear social function — protecting property, enabling trust, making communication reliable. From these cases one might propose: a rule is a social norm if and only if the majority conforms, violators are condemned, and the rule serves an identifiable social function.

Yet this definition, abstracted from norms of obvious social utility, proves too narrow. Consider the norm, once widespread in many Western societies, requiring women to wear hats in church. The norm was robustly observed: the great majority of women wore hats, and those who did not were subjected to disapproving stares, whispered reproach, and, in some congregations, outright exclusion. The first two conditions are met. But the third is doubtful in the extreme. What social function did the hat-wearing norm serve? It did not protect anyone's interests; it did not enable cooperation or coordination; it did not contribute to any identifiable collective benefit. It was a norm of pure convention, sustained by the inertia of custom and the pressure of social disapproval. The background cases were all norms with transparent social functions, and the definition assumed that social function was essential to normativity. But the hat-wearing case reveals that a norm may be fully operative — widely observed, socially enforced, and keenly felt — without serving any function at all. The definition has confused the explanation for why certain norms arise with the conditions under which a rule counts as a norm. A rule is a social norm when the group conforms to it and enforces conformity; the question of whether the norm is beneficial is a further question, and the answer may well be that it is not.

background_cases: Two farmers who agree to help each other with the harvest cooperate: each understands the arrangement, each contributes labour to the other's fields, and the agreement is explicit and mutually acknowledged.

A string quartet cooperates: each musician plays a designated part, each listens to and adjusts to the others, and the cooperation is guided by a shared understanding of the score and the conventions of ensemble performance.

Two scientists who collaborate on a research paper cooperate: each contributes expertise, each is aware of the other's contributions, and the collaboration is governed by an explicit agreement about the division of labour and the sharing of credit.
definition: Two or more agents cooperate if and only if (i) each agent is aware that they are participating in a joint activity, (ii) each agent intentionally contributes to a shared goal, and (iii) the agents have communicated with one another about their respective roles.
conditions: Each agent is aware of participating in a joint activity
Each agent intentionally contributes to a shared goal
The agents have communicated about their respective roles
counterexample: Consider two drivers approaching each other on a narrow country lane. Each slows down and pulls to the left to allow the other to pass. Neither has communicated with the other; they have never met, and no word or signal passes between them beyond, perhaps, a brief nod of acknowledgement. Yet each is aware that they are participating in a joint activity — the negotiation of a narrow passage — and each intentionally contributes to the shared goal of avoiding a collision. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not: there has been no communication about respective roles. The drivers rely on a shared convention (driving on the left) rather than on any explicit communication. Yet their interaction is a clear instance of cooperation — indeed, it is cooperation of the most spontaneous and effective kind.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the farmers, the quartet, the scientists — all involved explicit communication about roles, and the definition generalised from this to require such communication. But the drivers on the narrow lane cooperate without communication, relying instead on shared conventions and mutual adjustment. What the background cases truly shared was not explicit communication but mutual responsiveness in the service of a shared goal. The definition has mistaken a feature of planned, deliberate cooperation for a feature of cooperation as such, overlooking the spontaneous cooperation that arises when agents draw on shared conventions without exchanging a single word.
passage:
The farmers who help each other with the harvest, the musicians in a string quartet, the scientists collaborating on a paper — these are paradigmatic instances of cooperation. In each case, the agents are aware of the joint activity, each contributes intentionally to a shared goal, and the agents have communicated about their roles. From these cases one might extract the definition: agents cooperate if and only if each is aware of the joint activity, each contributes to a shared goal, and the agents have communicated about their roles.

Yet this definition, abstracted from cases of planned and articulated cooperation, excludes a vast domain of cooperative activity that arises spontaneously and without communication. Consider two drivers approaching each other on a narrow country lane. Each slows down and pulls to the left. Neither has spoken to the other; no word, no gesture, no signal passes between them beyond the movement of the vehicles themselves. Yet each is aware that they are engaged in a joint activity — the negotiation of a tight space — and each intentionally contributes to the shared goal of passing without collision. The first two conditions are met. But the third is not: no communication about roles has occurred. The drivers rely on a shared convention — the rule of the road — and on their mutual observation of each other's movements. Their cooperation is spontaneous, wordless, and entirely effective. The background cases all involved planned cooperation in which roles were discussed and allocated, and the definition assumed that such communication was essential. But what made those cases cooperative was not the communication as such but the mutual responsiveness of agents pursuing a shared goal. The drivers on the narrow lane exhibit precisely this mutual responsiveness. The definition has confused a common mechanism of cooperation — explicit role-assignment — with a necessary condition, overlooking the cooperation that arises silently, through shared conventions and the alert responsiveness of agents to one another's actions.

background_cases: A medieval English village was a community in the fullest sense: its members shared a location, interacted daily, and held a common stock of customs, beliefs, and memories that distinguished them from neighbouring villages. Each member's identity was bound up with the collective identity of the village.

A monastic order constitutes a community: its members share a common dwelling, engage in daily communal activities — prayer, labour, study — and are united by a shared rule of life and a common spiritual purpose.

A fishing village on the Scottish coast constitutes a community: its members share the same harbour, depend upon the same waters, and are united by a common livelihood, shared dangers, and a body of local lore transmitted across generations.
definition: A group G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G are united by a shared purpose or way of life, (ii) the members of G participate in common practices and institutions, and (iii) the members of G have a sense of mutual belonging.
conditions: Members are united by a shared purpose or way of life
Members participate in common practices and institutions
Members have a sense of mutual belonging
counterexample: Consider the passengers on a lifeboat following a shipwreck. They are united by a shared purpose — survival. They participate in common practices — rationing water, taking turns at the oars, keeping watch. And they develop a vivid sense of mutual belonging, born of shared peril and mutual dependence. All three conditions are met. Yet one might hesitate to call the occupants of a lifeboat a community. Their association is temporary, involuntary, and born of crisis rather than of any deeper commonality of life or aspiration. When rescued, they disperse and may never see one another again.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the village, the monastery, the fishing settlement — are all characterised by duration, voluntariness or deep cultural entrenchment, and the expectation of ongoing association. The definition captures shared purpose, common practices, and belonging but omits the temporal depth and the expectation of continuity that distinguish genuine communities from temporary associations forged by circumstance. The lifeboat case satisfies the definition precisely because the definition mistakes the synchronic features of community for the whole, ignoring the diachronic dimension — the shared past and the expectation of a shared future — that the background cases all exhibit.
passage:
The groups most confidently identified as communities share a temporal depth that is easy to overlook. A medieval English village had a shared past stretching back generations and an expectation of shared continuity into the future. A monastic order had a history embodied in its rule and its traditions, and its members expected the order to persist beyond their individual lifetimes. A Scottish fishing village had lore, customs, and memories that linked the living to the dead and the yet unborn. In each case, the community existed not merely in the present moment but across time — it had a past that informed its identity and a future toward which its members oriented their common life.

One might attempt to define a community as a group united by a shared purpose, participating in common practices, and possessing a sense of mutual belonging. This captures the synchronic features of the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the company of a lifeboat. When a ship goes down, the survivors in the lifeboat are suddenly united by the most urgent shared purpose imaginable — survival. They participate in common practices: rationing provisions, manning the oars, keeping watch for passing vessels. And they develop, often with extraordinary rapidity, a powerful sense of mutual belonging — a bond forged in shared peril. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet it seems strained to call the occupants of a lifeboat a community. Their association is temporary and involuntary. It was born of catastrophe, not of any commonality of life, culture, or aspiration. When rescue comes, the group dissolves, and its members return to their separate lives. The background cases all possessed what the lifeboat lacks: temporal depth, the expectation of continuity, and a shared history that gives the group an identity persisting beyond any single episode. The definition captures the present-tense features of community but omits the diachronic dimension that distinguishes a community from a crisis-born association.

background_cases: The norm of queuing in British society functions as a social norm because it prescribes an order of service, the majority of people conform to it, and those who violate it are subject to expressions of displeasure — tutting, sharp remarks, pointed stares — from fellow queuers.

The norm of reciprocating hospitality in rural communities functions as a social norm because it prescribes a pattern of social exchange, most members observe it, and those who accept hospitality without returning it are spoken of unfavourably.

The norm of removing one's hat indoors functioned as a social norm in earlier generations because it prescribed a form of decorum, most men observed it, and those who kept their hats on were regarded as boorish.
definition: A rule R is a social norm in a society S if and only if (i) R prescribes or proscribes a pattern of behaviour, (ii) the majority of members of S conform to R, and (iii) violations of R are met with informal social sanctions — disapproval, criticism, or exclusion.
conditions: R prescribes or proscribes behaviour
The majority conforms
Violations are met with informal sanctions
counterexample: Consider the norm against bribery in a society where bribery is in fact widespread. The norm proscribes bribery. Violations are met with informal sanctions — those known to accept bribes are spoken of with contempt, and public discourse consistently condemns the practice. Yet the majority of members do not conform; bribery is the routine method by which business is conducted. The second condition is not met. But the norm persists as a genuine social force: it shapes public discourse, it generates guilt in those who violate it, and it provides the standard by which reformers demand change. To say that the norm does not exist because majority compliance has failed seems to misdescribe the social reality.
abductive_insight: The background cases — queuing, reciprocating hospitality, removing hats — are all norms in which majority compliance and informal sanctioning co-occur. The definition treats majority conformity as necessary. But the bribery case shows that a norm may persist as a genuine prescriptive force even when it is more honoured in the breach than in the observance. The definition mistakes the co-occurrence of conformity and normativity in the background cases for a dependency, when what actually constitutes the norm is the persistence of the prescriptive standard and the practice of invoking it, whether or not the majority complies.
passage:
The social norms most readily identified share a feature that invites premature generalisation. The norm of queuing in Britain is observed by the great majority and enforced by informal sanctions. The norm of reciprocating hospitality in rural communities is widely followed and its violation earns reproach. The norm of removing one's hat indoors, in the generations when it was operative, was generally observed and its violation was regarded as a social failing. In each case, majority compliance and informal sanctioning are both present, and it is natural to regard both as necessary conditions of a social norm.

One might therefore define a social norm as a rule prescribing or proscribing behaviour to which the majority conforms and whose violation is informally sanctioned. This captures the structure of the background cases. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition is provided by the norm against bribery in a society where bribery is endemic. In many such societies, the norm against bribery is real and forceful: it is invoked in public discourse, it shapes the rhetoric of political campaigns, it generates genuine guilt in those who participate in corrupt exchanges, and it provides the evaluative standard by which reformers call for change. Violations are met with informal sanctions — those known to be corrupt are spoken of with contempt, even by people who are themselves corrupt. Yet the majority does not conform. Bribery is the routine mechanism by which permits are obtained, contracts awarded, and disputes settled. The second condition of the definition is not met. But to deny that the norm against bribery exists in this society is to ignore a powerful feature of its social life. The background cases exhibited majority conformity alongside normativity, and the definition treated both as necessary. The bribery case shows that normativity can persist without conformity — that a norm's existence consists in the persistence of the prescriptive standard and the practice of invoking it, not in the statistical fact of compliance.

background_cases: A patient trusts her physician because the physician has demonstrated competence over many consultations, has acted consistently in the patient's interest, and belongs to a profession regulated by standards of conduct. The trust is grounded in a track record of reliable performance.

A depositor trusts his bank because the bank has honoured withdrawals reliably, is regulated by state authorities, and has a long institutional history of solvency. The trust is grounded in demonstrated institutional reliability.

A villager trusts her neighbour because the neighbour has kept his word on many occasions, has assisted her in times of difficulty, and is embedded in a web of mutual acquaintance that would make defection costly. The trust is grounded in repeated interaction and social embeddedness.
definition: A person A rationally trusts an agent B with respect to a domain D if and only if (i) A has evidence that B has performed reliably in D in the past, (ii) A has no evidence that B's circumstances have changed in a way that would impair B's reliability, and (iii) A's reliance on B is proportionate to the strength of the available evidence.
conditions: A has evidence of B's past reliability in D
A has no evidence of changed circumstances impairing reliability
A's reliance is proportionate to the evidence
counterexample: Consider the first patient to consult a newly qualified physician. The patient has no evidence of the physician's past reliability — the physician has no track record. She has no evidence that the physician's circumstances have changed, but only because there are no prior circumstances to have changed. Yet the patient trusts the physician, and her trust is not irrational: she relies upon the physician's training, the regulatory framework that licenses medical practice, and the general competence of the profession. The first condition is not met, yet the trust is widely regarded as reasonable.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the established physician, the reliable bank, the trustworthy neighbour — all involve agents with demonstrated track records. The definition captures this track-record basis for trust. But the case of the newly qualified physician shows that rational trust may be extended in the absence of any individual track record, on the basis of institutional credentials, professional training, and systemic safeguards. The definition mistakes the particular evidential basis of the paradigm cases — direct experience of past reliability — for the only legitimate basis, when trust may also be rationally grounded in the institutional and systemic structures that vouch for an agent who has not yet had the opportunity to demonstrate individual reliability.
passage:
The cases in which trust is most obviously rational share an evidential basis that is easy to mistake for a necessary condition. A patient trusts her established physician on the strength of years of competent care. A depositor trusts his bank on the strength of its long history of honouring withdrawals. A villager trusts her neighbour on the strength of repeated interactions in which he has kept his word. In each case, the trust is underwritten by a track record — a body of evidence accumulated through direct experience of the trusted agent's past performance.

One might therefore define rational trust as reliance grounded in evidence of past reliability, undisturbed by evidence of changed circumstances, and proportionate to the strength of the evidence. This captures the evidential structure of the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition is provided by the situation of the first patient to consult a newly qualified physician. The physician has just completed her training and has no professional track record. The patient possesses no evidence of the physician's past reliability, for there is no past to draw upon. The first condition of the definition is not met. Yet the patient's trust is not irrational. She relies upon the fact that the physician has been trained at a recognised medical school, has passed qualifying examinations, and is licensed by a regulatory authority whose function is to ensure competence. Her trust is grounded not in individual experience but in institutional structures that vouch for the physician's competence. The background cases all involved trust built upon personal acquaintance with a track record; the definition treats this as the only rational basis. But the newly qualified physician case shows that rational trust may be extended on systemic and institutional grounds in the absence of any individual track record, and a definition that excludes this possibility is too narrow.

background_cases: The authority of a judge rests upon the legal system that appoints him, the body of law that defines his powers, and the institutional mechanisms — courts, bailiffs, prisons — that enforce his decisions. His authority is constituted by the institution and ceases when the institution withdraws its recognition.

The authority of a military officer rests upon the chain of command that placed him in his position, the regulations that define his powers, and the disciplinary mechanisms that enforce obedience. His authority is constituted by the military institution and would vanish if the institution ceased to recognise his rank.

The authority of a headmaster rests upon the school that employs him, the rules that define his powers over pupils and staff, and the institutional mechanisms — expulsion, dismissal — that enforce his decisions. His authority is constituted by the school and ceases when his appointment ends.
definition: A person A has authority over a group G if and only if (i) A's authority is conferred by an institution that G recognises as legitimate, (ii) A's directives fall within the scope defined by the institution, and (iii) the institution provides mechanisms for enforcing A's directives.
conditions: A's authority is conferred by a recognised institution
A's directives fall within the institutionally defined scope
The institution provides enforcement mechanisms
counterexample: Consider a revolutionary leader in a country undergoing a political upheaval. The old institutions have collapsed — the courts are closed, the military has fragmented, the government has fled. No institution confers authority upon the revolutionary leader, for there are no functioning institutions. No institutional scope defines his directives, for no institutional framework exists. No enforcement mechanisms are provided by any institution. Yet the revolutionary leader commands obedience from thousands of followers who carry out his directives willingly and effectively. His authority arises not from any institution but from his personal qualities — his charisma, his clarity of purpose, his willingness to act when others hesitate.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the judge, the officer, the headmaster — all exercise authority within stable institutional frameworks. The definition captures the institutional character of their authority but cannot accommodate the authority that arises in the absence of institutions, as in revolutions, where a leader commands obedience through personal qualities rather than institutional conferral. Authority may be institutional or personal, and the definition recognises only the first variety.
passage:
The authorities most familiar in settled social life share a common origin in institutions. The judge derives his authority from the legal system: it is the law that empowers him to decide cases, the court that gives him a bench from which to pronounce, and the apparatus of enforcement that ensures his decisions are carried out. The military officer derives his authority from the chain of command: it is the army that confers his rank and the discipline of the service that ensures his orders are obeyed. The headmaster derives his authority from the school: it is the institution that appoints him and defines the scope of his power over pupils and staff. In each case, the authority is conferred by an institution, exercised within an institutionally defined scope, and supported by institutional mechanisms of enforcement.

One might codify these observations by defining authority as requiring institutional conferral, institutional scope, and institutional enforcement. This definition captures the background cases precisely. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the authority wielded during a revolutionary upheaval. Consider a country in which the old regime has collapsed. The courts are closed, the army has dissolved into factions, the government has fled across the border. No institutions function; the framework within which authority was formerly exercised has been swept away. In this vacuum, a revolutionary leader emerges. He commands the loyalty and obedience of thousands. His directives are carried out with speed and precision. Yet no institution confers his authority — there are no functioning institutions. No institutional scope defines the limits of his power — there are no rules. No enforcement mechanism is provided by any institution — there is no apparatus of enforcement beyond the willingness of his followers to act. His authority rests entirely upon personal qualities: the force of his character, the clarity of his vision, the confidence he inspires. The background cases all operated within stable institutional frameworks. The definition made institutional conferral a necessary condition. But the revolutionary case demonstrates that authority can arise in the complete absence of institutions, rooted in personal qualities that command obedience directly, without the mediation of any institutional structure.

background_cases: The distribution of prizes at a school awards ceremony is just when it is based on merit: the prize for mathematics goes to the pupil who performed best in the examinations, and the prize for athletics goes to the fastest runner. Each recipient receives a reward proportional to demonstrated achievement.

The distribution of pay among workers in a factory is just when it reflects the skill and effort of each worker: the skilled machinist earns more than the unskilled labourer because his work requires greater training and produces greater value.

The distribution of grades among university students is just when it reflects the quality of their work: the student who writes the most thorough and original essay receives the highest mark, and the student whose work is perfunctory receives a low mark.
definition: A distribution of goods among a group of persons is just if and only if (i) the goods are distributed on the basis of merit — that is, each person receives a share proportional to their demonstrated achievement or contribution, (ii) the criteria of merit are applied consistently to all persons, and (iii) the criteria of merit are publicly known in advance of the distribution.
conditions: Goods are distributed proportionally to demonstrated merit
Criteria of merit are applied consistently to all
Criteria are publicly known in advance
counterexample: Consider the distribution of organs for transplantation among patients waiting for a kidney. The organs are not distributed on the basis of merit: a patient who has contributed greatly to society does not receive priority over a patient who has contributed little. The distribution is based on medical urgency, compatibility, and time spent waiting — criteria that have nothing to do with achievement or desert. Yet the distribution is widely regarded as just, and a distribution based on merit — giving kidneys first to those who have achieved the most — would be regarded as a grotesque violation of justice.
abductive_insight: The background cases — school prizes, factory wages, university grades — all involve contexts where the goods distributed are rewards for performance, and merit is the natural basis for distribution. The definition captures the justice of reward-based distributions but conflates one species of justice with the whole genus. In contexts where the goods distributed are necessities — medical care, basic sustenance, emergency relief — justice requires distribution according to need, and merit is not merely irrelevant but would be positively unjust as a criterion.
passage:
The distributions that seem most obviously just share a common principle: each person receives what he has earned. At the school awards ceremony, the mathematics prize goes to the pupil who solved the most problems, and no one complains. In the factory, the skilled machinist earns more than the unskilled labourer, and the difference is accepted as fair. At the university, the student who writes the best essay receives the highest mark. In each case, the goods are distributed on the basis of merit, the criteria are applied consistently, and the criteria are known in advance. Merit-based distribution, in these contexts, is just.

One might codify these observations by defining a just distribution as one based on merit, applied consistently, with publicly known criteria. The definition accommodates the background cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the distribution of scarce medical resources. Consider the allocation of kidneys for transplantation. Patients wait on a list, sometimes for years. When a kidney becomes available, it is allocated not to the patient who has achieved the most or contributed the most to society but to the patient who is the best medical match, who has waited the longest, or whose condition is most urgent. The criteria have nothing to do with merit, achievement, or desert. A criminal and a saint with identical medical profiles are treated identically. The distribution violates all three conditions of the definition: it is not based on merit, the criteria of merit are not applied (for there are none), and what is publicly known is a set of medical and temporal criteria that the definition does not recognise as relevant. Yet the distribution is widely regarded as just — indeed, as the only just way to allocate organs.
