A distribution that gave kidneys to the most meritorious patients would be regarded not as just but as monstrous. The background cases all involved goods that were rewards for performance — prizes, wages, grades. The definition made merit the basis of justice because merit was the appropriate basis in those contexts. But the allocation of medical resources is not a reward; it is a response to need, and justice in this context requires attention to need rather than to merit.

background_cases: A prisoner is unfree because he is physically confined: the walls, the locks, and the guards prevent him from leaving. His unfreedom consists in the presence of external, physical barriers to movement that another agent has deliberately imposed.

A person under a curfew is unfree to walk the streets at night because the government has prohibited nocturnal movement and stationed patrols to enforce the prohibition. His unfreedom consists in a legal restriction backed by the threat of arrest.

A person who has been forbidden by an employer to speak to journalists is unfree to speak because the employer has threatened dismissal if the prohibition is violated. His unfreedom consists in a restriction imposed by another agent and backed by a sanction.
definition: A person A is unfree with respect to an action X if and only if (i) there exists an external obstacle to A's performing X, (ii) the obstacle has been imposed or maintained by another agent or agents, and (iii) the obstacle is such that A cannot overcome it by normal effort.
conditions: An external obstacle to A's performing X exists
The obstacle has been imposed by another agent
The obstacle cannot be overcome by normal effort
counterexample: Consider a person who has a severe and irrational fear of open spaces — agoraphobia. She is physically able to leave her house; no wall, no guard, no legal prohibition prevents her. No other agent has imposed or maintains any obstacle. She could, in purely physical terms, open the door and walk into the street. Yet she cannot bring herself to do it. Her fear is so intense and so involuntary that it constitutes, for practical purposes, an insuperable barrier to leaving the house. She is unfree to go outside, yet her unfreedom does not consist in any external obstacle imposed by another agent. It consists in an internal psychological condition that she did not choose and cannot overcome by an act of will.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the prisoner, the person under curfew, the silenced employee — all involve unfreedom produced by external obstacles deliberately imposed by other agents. The definition captures this external, agent-imposed character of unfreedom but cannot accommodate cases in which a person is rendered unfree by internal conditions — phobias, addictions, compulsions — that are no less constraining than prison walls. Unfreedom may arise from within as well as from without, and a definition that recognises only external, agent-imposed obstacles is too narrow.
passage:
The cases of unfreedom that seem most clear-cut share a common structure: another agent has placed an obstacle in the path of the person's action. The prisoner cannot leave because the state has built walls and stationed guards. The person under curfew cannot walk at night because the government has imposed a prohibition. The employee cannot speak to journalists because the employer has threatened dismissal. In each case, the obstacle is external, it has been imposed by a specific agent, and it cannot be overcome by ordinary effort.

One might codify these observations by defining unfreedom as the presence of an external obstacle, imposed by another agent, that cannot be overcome by normal effort. The definition captures the background cases precisely and excludes cases where a person merely lacks the ability to do something — one is not unfree to fly merely because one lacks wings. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is the unfreedom produced by severe psychological conditions. Consider a woman who has agoraphobia — an intense, irrational fear of open spaces. She has not been imprisoned. No government has confined her to her house. No employer, no relative, no authority of any kind has imposed any restriction upon her movements. She is physically strong, her door is unlocked, and the street is open before her. Yet she cannot leave her house. Every time she approaches the threshold, a terror so overwhelming that it amounts to a physical impossibility seizes her. She has tried — she has tried many times — and she cannot overcome it. No external obstacle exists, and no agent has imposed or maintains one. The first and second conditions of the definition both fail. Yet she is unfree in a sense that is no less real, and no less constraining, than the unfreedom of the prisoner. Her agoraphobia confines her as effectively as any cell. The background cases all involved external, agent-imposed obstacles, and the definition made these features necessary. But unfreedom may also arise from internal conditions — phobias, addictions, compulsions — that constrain a person's actions as thoroughly as any external barrier, and a definition that recognises only external obstacles is blind to this dimension of human unfreedom.

background_cases: A fishing village on the coast of Cornwall constitutes a community: its members share the same harbour, interact daily at the market and the pub, observe shared customs of seamanship and mutual aid, and possess a collective identity forged by generations of common life in the same place.

A monastic order constitutes a community: its members live together within the walls of the monastery, follow a shared rule of life, interact constantly in prayer, labour, and meals, and identify themselves as members of a single body with a common purpose.

A neighbourhood in a large city constitutes a community: its residents share the same streets and parks, patronise the same shops, send their children to the same school, and participate in a network of informal mutual assistance — watching each other's houses, lending household items, exchanging greetings.
definition: A group of persons G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G share a common physical environment — a territory, a neighbourhood, or a dwelling, (ii) the members of G engage in face-to-face interaction on a regular basis, and (iii) the members of G share a sense of collective identity — a consciousness of belonging to the group.
conditions: Members share a common physical environment
Members engage in regular face-to-face interaction
Members share a sense of collective identity
counterexample: Consider a community of scholars who study a rare and ancient language — Hittite, say. There are perhaps forty such scholars in the world, scattered across universities in a dozen countries. They have never all been in the same room. They share no common territory or physical environment. Their face-to-face interactions are infrequent — a conference every few years, the occasional visit to a colleague's university. Yet they possess a profound sense of collective identity. They read each other's work with care; they correspond regularly; they feel a bond of shared purpose and mutual recognition that distinguishes them from the wider academic world. They refer to themselves as a community, and others refer to them in the same way.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the fishing village, the monastery, the neighbourhood — all involve groups whose members share a physical space and interact face to face as a matter of daily routine. The definition captures the territorial and embodied character of these communities but cannot accommodate communities constituted by shared intellectual purpose, professional identity, or cultural practice, in which the members may be widely dispersed and rarely meet in person yet experience a powerful sense of belonging and mutual recognition.
passage:
The groups that serve as paradigms of community share a territorial and embodied character. The fishing village is bound together by the harbour, the daily catch, and the customs of the sea. The monastery is bound together by its walls, the shared rule, and the rhythm of communal prayer. The neighbourhood is bound together by shared streets, the local school, and the informal networks of mutual assistance. In each case, the members share a physical environment, engage in face-to-face interaction as a matter of course, and possess a vivid sense of collective identity.

One might therefore define community as a group whose members share a common physical environment, interact face to face on a regular basis, and share a sense of collective identity. The definition accommodates the background cases well. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the scholarly community. There are, perhaps, forty scholars in the world who devote their professional lives to the study of Hittite — an ancient Anatolian language preserved in cuneiform tablets. These scholars are scattered across universities in a dozen countries. They share no physical environment; their face-to-face interactions are rare — a conference here, a visiting lectureship there. Yet they constitute a community in every sense that matters. They read each other's work with intense attention; they correspond regularly and at length; they share a sense of collective purpose and mutual recognition that binds them together across distance and national boundary. They speak of themselves as a community, and the wider academic world recognises them as one. The background cases were all territorial and embodied, and the definition captured this character as essential. But community can be constituted by shared intellectual purpose and mutual recognition without any shared territory or regular face-to-face contact.

background_cases: A judge has authority because she holds an office established by law, has been appointed through a constitutionally defined process, and exercises power within the bounds of her jurisdiction. Her authority is legitimate because it derives from a recognised institutional framework that the community has accepted.

A military officer has authority because he holds a rank conferred by the armed forces, has been commissioned through a formal procedure, and commands within the limits of military law. His authority derives from an institutional structure that predates him and will survive his departure.

A headmaster has authority because she holds a position created by the school's governing body, has been appointed through an established process, and exercises disciplinary and administrative power within the bounds of the school's regulations. Her authority derives from the institutional framework of the school.
definition: A person P has authority over a group G if and only if (i) P's authority derives from an institutional framework that the members of G accept as legitimate, (ii) P has been installed in their position through a process recognised as proper by that framework, and (iii) P exercises their authority within the bounds set by the framework.
conditions: P's authority derives from an accepted institutional framework
P was installed through a process recognised as proper
P exercises authority within the bounds set by the framework
counterexample: Consider a charismatic preacher who arrives in a small town and, through the force of his oratory, his apparent moral vision, and his personal magnetism, acquires a following. He holds no office; no institution has appointed him; no framework defines the bounds of his influence. Yet the townspeople defer to his judgement, follow his counsel, and organise their communal life around his pronouncements. His authority is real and effective — more effective, perhaps, than that of the town's elected officials. But none of the three conditions is satisfied: there is no institutional framework, no recognised process of appointment, and no defined bounds. His authority is personal rather than institutional.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the judge, the officer, the headmaster — all involve authority that is mediated by established institutions: courts, armies, schools. The definition captures the institutional mediation that characterises these forms of authority but cannot accommodate the authority that arises from personal qualities — charisma, moral vision, rhetorical power — and that operates outside any institutional framework. What the background cases all possessed — an institutional scaffolding — the definition has taken as the sole source of authority, overlooking the ancient and powerful form of authority that flows directly from the person.
passage:
The figures whose authority seems most secure and most readily analysable share a common institutional scaffolding. The judge derives her authority from the legal system — a vast institutional framework of constitutions, statutes, and precedents that defines her office, prescribes the process of her appointment, and limits her jurisdiction. The military officer derives his authority from the armed forces — an institution with its own hierarchy, its own procedures of commissioning, and its own body of law. The headmaster derives her authority from the school's governing structure. In each case, the authority is institutional: it exists because a framework exists, it is conferred through a process the framework defines, and it is exercised within bounds the framework sets.

One might therefore define authority as a power that derives from an accepted institutional framework, conferred through a recognised process, and exercised within defined bounds. This definition accommodates the background cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the authority of the charismatic leader. Consider a preacher who arrives in a small town, without office, without appointment, without any institutional affiliation. Through the power of his speech, the clarity of his moral vision, and the force of his personality, he acquires a devoted following. The townspeople consult him in their difficulties, defer to his judgements, and organise aspects of their communal life around his guidance. His authority is real — arguably more potent than that of the town's elected council. But it derives from no institutional framework. No process has installed him in a position. No defined bounds constrain his influence. His authority is personal, arising from qualities of character and rhetoric rather than from the scaffolding of institutions. The background cases all involved institutionally mediated authority, and the definition has treated institutional mediation as a necessary condition. But authority can arise from the person alone, without any institutional support, and the concept must accommodate this possibility.

background_cases: The tradition of English common law has been transmitted from generation to generation of jurists, each learning the principles and precedents from their predecessors, each applying them in new cases whilst preserving their essential character. The transmission is deliberate — law students are trained, barristers are apprenticed, judges are schooled in the existing body of decisions.

The tradition of classical music performance has been transmitted from master to pupil across centuries, each generation learning the techniques of intonation, phrasing, and interpretation from the preceding one, each preserving the core practices whilst adapting them to new instruments and concert halls.

The tradition of a family recipe for Christmas pudding has been transmitted from mother to daughter through five generations, each cook learning the method by watching and helping, each preserving the essential ingredients and proportions whilst perhaps adjusting for the tastes of the day.
definition: A practice P constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted across at least three generations, (ii) the transmission has been deliberate — each generation has intentionally taught P to the next, and (iii) the practitioners regard P as authoritative precisely because of its age and its connection to the past.
conditions: P has been transmitted across at least three generations
The transmission has been deliberate and intentional
Practitioners regard P as authoritative because of its age and connection to the past
counterexample: Consider the practice of jaywalking in a particular city — crossing the road at points other than designated crossings, weaving between traffic, timing one's passage by the rhythm of the traffic lights. This practice has been followed for generations. But its transmission is not deliberate: no one teaches it intentionally. New residents and young people acquire it by observation and imitation, absorbing the habit from the behaviour of those around them without explicit instruction. The second condition fails. Yet the practice has the character of a tradition — it is deeply embedded in the city's culture, it is passed from generation to generation, and long-term residents would regard its disappearance as a loss of something characteristically their own.
abductive_insight: The background cases — common law, classical performance, the family recipe — all involve traditions that are transmitted through deliberate, intentional teaching: the law student is trained, the musician is instructed, the cook is shown the method. The definition captures this deliberate, pedagogical character of transmission but cannot accommodate traditions that are transmitted tacitly, through imitation and absorption rather than through explicit instruction. Many of the most deeply rooted traditions — patterns of speech, styles of walking, habits of social interaction — are acquired not by teaching but by living among those who practise them.
passage:
The traditions that come most readily to mind as clear instances of the concept share a feature of deliberate, intentional transmission. The tradition of English common law is transmitted through a structured process of legal education: students read cases, attend lectures, serve apprenticeships. The tradition of classical music performance is transmitted from master to pupil: the student learns bowing technique, phrasing, and interpretive style through years of individual instruction. The family recipe is transmitted by a mother showing her daughter how to mix the batter, demonstrating the proper consistency, explaining when to add the brandy. In each case, the transmission is intentional — each generation deliberately teaches the next.

One might therefore define a tradition as a practice transmitted across at least three generations through deliberate teaching, regarded by its practitioners as authoritative because of its age and connection to the past. The definition accommodates the background cases well. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the traditions that are transmitted not by deliberate teaching but by tacit imitation. Consider the practice of jaywalking in a busy city. Long-term residents cross roads at unmarked points, weaving through traffic with a confidence born of lifelong habit. The practice has been followed for generations. New residents acquire it not through instruction but through observation — watching how others cross, absorbing the timing, the rhythm, the unspoken rules of when it is safe to dart between vehicles. No one deliberately teaches jaywalking; it is learned by living in the city. The second condition of the definition fails. Yet the practice has the quality of a tradition: it is deeply characteristic of the city, it is transmitted from generation to generation, and its disappearance would be felt as a cultural loss. The background cases all involved deliberate transmission; the definition treated this as essential. But many traditions — indeed, many of the most deeply embedded — are transmitted through absorption rather than instruction.

background_cases: A small fishing village on the coast of Cornwall constitutes a community: its members live in close proximity, interact daily, share customs and dialect, and cooperate in the management of the harbour and the distribution of the catch. The community is bound by geography, kinship, and shared livelihood.

The Jewish diaspora communities of mediaeval Europe constituted genuine communities despite being scattered across many cities and countries. Their unity derived from shared religious practice, shared sacred texts, a common language of worship, and networks of correspondence and mutual aid that transcended geographical boundaries.

The Quaker community in seventeenth-century England was bound not by geography or kinship but by shared religious conviction, shared practices of worship, and a distinctive ethical code. Members recognised one another as belonging to the same community and offered mutual support.
definition: A group of persons G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G share a common identity that they themselves recognise, (ii) the members of G interact with one another more frequently than with outsiders, and (iii) the members of G share a set of norms or values that govern their interactions with one another.
conditions: The members of G share a common identity that they themselves recognise
The members of G interact with one another more frequently than with outsiders
The members of G share a set of norms or values that govern their interactions with one another
counterexample: Consider the community of speakers of a dying language — say, the last several hundred speakers of a particular indigenous tongue, scattered across a large country and integrated into the dominant linguistic culture. Each speaker recognises herself as belonging to the community of speakers of this language. Each shares the norms of the language — its grammar, its idioms, its proprieties of address. Yet the speakers may interact with one another far less frequently than they interact with speakers of the dominant language; they may meet only once or twice a year at cultural gatherings. The second condition fails. Yet one might observe that these speakers constitute a genuine linguistic community, and that their communal identity is sustained by the shared possession of the language itself rather than by the frequency of their mutual interaction.
abductive_insight: The background cases reveal different bases of communal unity: the fishing village is bound by geography and daily interaction; the Jewish diaspora by religious practice and mutual aid; the Quakers by conviction and shared worship. The dying-language case introduces a community whose unity derives from a shared cultural possession — the language itself — even in the near-absence of regular interaction. What the background cases and the language case jointly suggest is that community can be constituted by shared cultural inheritance as much as by interaction, and that the frequency of interaction is a common accompaniment of community rather than a necessary condition of it.
passage:
The concept of community admits of considerable variation in its grounds and manifestations. A Cornish fishing village is a community bound by geographical proximity, daily interaction, and shared livelihood. The Jewish communities of mediaeval Europe were bound by shared religious practice and mutual aid across vast distances. The Quaker community of seventeenth-century England was constituted by shared conviction and a distinctive mode of worship. These background cases suggest that community may be grounded in geography, in religion, in shared practice, or in some combination of these.

One might attempt to codify these observations by defining a community as a group whose members share a recognised common identity, interact more frequently with one another than with outsiders, and share a set of governing norms or values. This definition captures the fishing village, the diaspora, and the Quakers with reasonable fidelity. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the phenomenon of dying languages. Consider the last several hundred speakers of a particular indigenous language, scattered across a large country and thoroughly integrated into the dominant linguistic culture. Each speaker recognises herself as belonging to the community of speakers of this language. Each shares the norms of the language — its grammar, its idioms, its delicate proprieties of address. Yet the speakers interact with one another far less frequently than they interact with speakers of the dominant language; they may meet only at an annual cultural gathering. The second condition fails decisively. Yet one might observe that these speakers constitute a genuine community — a linguistic community — and that their bond is sustained by the shared possession of the language itself. The background cases, taken together with the dying-language case, suggest that community can be constituted by shared cultural inheritance as much as by regular mutual interaction. The definition, by requiring frequency of interaction, mistakes a common feature of community for an essential one.

background_cases: The tradition of common law in England developed over centuries through the accumulation of judicial decisions. Each judge built upon the rulings of predecessors, adapting the law to new circumstances while maintaining a continuous thread of precedent. The tradition evolved constantly, yet each generation of lawyers regarded itself as participating in the same ongoing practice.

The tradition of classical music in the West evolved from mediaeval plainchant through Renaissance polyphony, Baroque counterpoint, Classical sonata form, and Romantic symphonic expansion. Each generation of composers inherited the practice of the preceding generation and transformed it, sometimes radically. Yet the tradition was recognised as a continuous one throughout.

The tradition of Japanese tea ceremony has been transmitted from master to pupil for centuries. Each generation introduces subtle variations in the arrangement of utensils, the choice of scrolls, and the style of the host's movements. Yet these variations take place within a framework of inherited practice that is recognisably continuous.
definition: A practice T constitutes a living tradition if and only if (i) T has been transmitted from practitioners to successors over at least three generations, (ii) the practitioners of T regard themselves as continuing a practice inherited from the past, and (iii) T retains a core of unchanged elements that give it its distinctive character.
conditions: T has been transmitted from practitioners to successors over at least three generations
The practitioners of T regard themselves as continuing a practice inherited from the past
T retains a core of unchanged elements that give it its distinctive character
counterexample: Consider the tradition of Western painting from the Renaissance to the present. The practice has been transmitted across many generations. Painters regard themselves as participating in an ongoing tradition of image-making inherited from their predecessors. Yet when one compares a painting by Giotto with a painting by Jackson Pollock, it is difficult to identify any core of unchanged elements: the materials, the techniques, the subject matter, the purposes, and the aesthetic principles have all been transformed beyond recognition. The third condition fails. Yet the tradition of Western painting is manifestly a living tradition — one of the richest and most continuous in the history of human civilisation.
abductive_insight: The background cases — common law, classical music, tea ceremony — all exhibit continuous transformation within a framework of inherited practice. But they differ in the degree to which a stable core persists. Common law preserves the principle of precedent while the substance of the law changes. Classical music preserves certain harmonic principles while forms, instrumentation, and expressive ideals shift. The tea ceremony preserves a ritual framework while details vary. The Western painting case pushes this pattern to its limit: the tradition is continuous, but no stable core of elements persists from Giotto to Pollock. What the background cases and the painting case jointly reveal is that a tradition's continuity may consist not in the retention of unchanging elements but in the chain of influence and response linking each generation to the last — a continuous process of inheritance, critique, and transformation.
passage:
The most enduring traditions share a feature that is easily overlooked: they are constituted not by stasis but by continuous transformation within a framework of inheritance. The common law of England evolved over centuries through the accumulation of judicial decisions, each building upon and sometimes departing from its predecessors. The tradition of Western classical music transformed itself from plainchant to symphony, with each generation inheriting and reshaping the practice of the last. The Japanese tea ceremony, transmitted from master to pupil across many generations, accommodates subtle variation within a stable ritual framework.

One might attempt to codify these observations by defining a living tradition as a practice transmitted over at least three generations, regarded by its practitioners as continuing an inherited practice, and retaining a core of unchanged elements that give it its distinctive character. This definition captures the tea ceremony, with its stable ritual framework, and classical music, with its harmonic principles. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the tradition of Western painting. The practice has been transmitted across dozens of generations. Painters from Giotto to Pollock have regarded themselves as participating in an ongoing tradition of image-making. Yet when one compares a fresco by Giotto with an action painting by Pollock, it is exceedingly difficult to identify any core of unchanged elements. The materials are different; the techniques are different; the subject matter is different; the aesthetic principles are different; the very purposes of painting have been transformed. The third condition fails. Yet the tradition of Western painting is manifestly a living tradition — indeed, one of the most vital in the history of civilisation. The background cases, taken together with the painting case, suggest that a tradition's continuity need not consist in the preservation of an unchanging core. It may consist, rather, in the chain of influence and critical response that links each generation to the preceding one — a continuous process of inheritance, critique, and transformation in which nothing remains unchanged except the practice of inheriting and transforming.

background_cases: In the New England town meeting of the seventeenth century, settlers gathered to deliberate upon and consent to the rules by which they would be governed. Each participant had a voice, and decisions were taken by majority vote. The social arrangement derived its authority from the actual, explicit consent of the governed.

The Mayflower Compact of 1620 was a written agreement signed by the male passengers of the Mayflower, binding themselves to a 'civil Body Politick' and pledging obedience to the laws they would collectively enact. The signatories expressly consented to the arrangement and understood its terms.

The constitutional convention that produced the United States Constitution of 1787 involved elected delegates who debated, drafted, and ratified a framework of government. The Constitution was then submitted to state conventions for ratification — a further layer of consent by designated representatives of the people.
definition: A political arrangement P is legitimate if and only if (i) P derives its authority from the consent of the governed, (ii) the consent is given freely and without coercion, and (iii) the governed have a genuine opportunity to withdraw their consent if the arrangement fails to serve their interests.
conditions: P derives its authority from the consent of the governed
The consent is given freely and without coercion
The governed have a genuine opportunity to withdraw their consent if the arrangement fails to serve their interests
counterexample: Consider a just and benevolent colonial administration that provides peace, security, impartial justice, and material prosperity to a colonised population. The population has not consented to the arrangement — it was imposed by conquest. There is no mechanism for withdrawing consent, for consent was never given. All three conditions fail. Yet suppose the population, surveyed honestly, expresses overwhelming satisfaction with the arrangement and has no desire to change it. One might observe that the arrangement, though lacking consent, provides genuine and appreciated benefits, and that its legitimacy — if it has any — rests on something other than the consent of the governed.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the town meeting, the Mayflower Compact, the constitutional convention — all derive their authority from the explicit consent of participants. The colonial administration case reveals that consent-based legitimacy cannot accommodate arrangements that are widely beneficial and widely accepted yet were never consented to. What the background cases share is not merely consent but a further feature: the governed are the authors of the arrangement, not merely its beneficiaries. The colonial case, by separating benefit from authorship, reveals that the definition conflates two distinct sources of legitimacy — the consent of the governed and the welfare of the governed — and that an arrangement may possess one without the other.
passage:
The paradigmatic instances of legitimate political authority derive from the explicit consent of the governed. In the New England town meeting, settlers gathered to deliberate and to consent to the rules by which they would live. The Mayflower Compact was a written agreement, freely signed, binding its signatories to a common political framework. The constitutional convention of 1787 produced a framework that was debated, drafted, and ratified by representatives of the people. In each background case, the political arrangement drew its authority from the actual, freely given consent of those who would live under it.

One might attempt to codify these observations by defining a legitimate political arrangement as one that derives its authority from the free consent of the governed and that permits the withdrawal of consent when the arrangement fails to serve their interests. This definition captures the town meeting, the compact, and the convention. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the phenomenon of benevolent colonial rule. Suppose a colonial administration, imposed by conquest and never consented to by the population, provides peace, impartial justice, material prosperity, and security. The population has no mechanism for withdrawing consent, because consent was never given. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet suppose that the population, surveyed with complete honesty, expresses overwhelming satisfaction with the arrangement and has no wish to see it changed. One might observe that the arrangement provides genuine and appreciated benefits, and that to deny it any form of legitimacy whatever seems to disregard the welfare of the very people whose interests the definition purports to protect. The background cases, taken together with the colonial case, reveal a tension within the concept of political legitimacy. The definition captures legitimacy-by-consent but cannot accommodate legitimacy-by-welfare — and the colonial case suggests that the two may come apart. An arrangement may be consented to without benefiting the governed, and it may benefit the governed without being consented to. The definition, by treating consent as the sole ground of legitimacy, cannot accommodate this divergence.

background_cases: When a group of labourers jointly harvest a field and divide the crop in proportion to each worker's effort, the distribution is regarded as just. The labourer who worked the longest receives the most; the one who worked least receives the least. The distribution tracks a morally relevant difference — the effort each person contributed — and is accepted as fair by the participants.

When a teacher distributes grades in proportion to the quality of each student's work, the distribution is regarded as just. The student who wrote the finest essay receives the highest mark; the one who submitted careless work receives a lower mark. The distribution tracks a morally relevant difference — the quality of the work — and is accepted as fair.

When a court awards damages to a plaintiff in proportion to the injury suffered, the distribution is regarded as just. The plaintiff who suffered greater harm receives greater compensation. The distribution tracks a morally relevant difference — the extent of the injury — and is accepted as fair by those who understand the case.
definition: A distribution D of goods among members of a group is just if and only if (i) D allocates goods in proportion to some morally relevant criterion, (ii) D applies that criterion consistently to all members, and (iii) D is the outcome of a procedure that all affected parties regard as fair.
conditions: D allocates goods proportionally to a morally relevant criterion
D applies the criterion consistently to all
D results from a procedure regarded as fair
counterexample: Consider a society that distributes access to medical care in proportion to each person's financial contribution to the health system. The criterion — financial contribution — is applied consistently to all citizens. The procedure — a transparent system of fees and entitlements — is widely regarded as fair by the majority. All three conditions are met. Yet a desperately ill person who has been unable to work owing to a lifelong disability, and who has therefore contributed nothing financially, receives no care. The distribution is proportional, consistent, and procedurally fair, yet it condemns an innocent person to suffering and death. One might question whether such a distribution is just.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the medical-care case: in each, the morally relevant criterion upon which the distribution is based is one that the recipients had a fair opportunity to satisfy. The labourers chose how much effort to invest. The students chose how carefully to compose their essays. The plaintiff's injury, though not chosen, is a factual matter that the court can assess independently. In the medical-care case, the criterion — financial contribution — excludes a person whose inability to contribute is involuntary. The distribution is proportional and consistent but inattentive to the question of whether the criterion itself is one that all members could reasonably be expected to satisfy. The definition captures proportionality, consistency, and procedural fairness, but omits the requirement that the criterion of distribution be one to which all affected parties had fair access.
passage:
The distributions most widely regarded as just share a structural feature beyond mere proportionality and procedural regularity. When labourers divide a harvest in proportion to effort, each worker had the opportunity to contribute more or less effort — the criterion is one that all could satisfy. When a teacher assigns grades in proportion to quality of work, each student had the opportunity to produce better or worse work — the criterion is within each student's control. When a court awards damages in proportion to injury, the assessment is based on a factual matter that the court evaluates impartially. In each case, the criterion upon which the distribution is based is one to which all affected parties had fair access.

One might attempt to characterise a just distribution as one that allocates goods in proportion to a morally relevant criterion, applies that criterion consistently, and results from a procedure regarded as fair. This definition captures the formal and procedural features of justice. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the distribution of medical care. Suppose a society allocates access to medical treatment in proportion to each citizen's financial contribution to the health system. The criterion is applied consistently. The procedure — a transparent schedule of fees and entitlements — is regarded as fair by the majority. All three conditions are met. Yet a citizen who has been unable to work owing to a lifelong disability, and who has therefore contributed nothing financially, receives no care. She is condemned to suffer and die by a distribution that is proportional, consistent, and procedurally fair. What the background cases possess and the medical-care case lacks is a criterion to which all affected parties had a fair opportunity to respond. The labourers could choose to work harder; the students could choose to write more carefully. The disabled citizen could not choose to earn more. The definition attends to the structure and procedure of distribution but not to the fairness of the criterion itself — whether it is one that all members could reasonably have been expected to satisfy.

background_cases: The authority of a judge in a court of law derives from a system of legal institutions — the judge is appointed through established procedures, exercises powers defined by statute, and renders decisions that are enforceable because the legal system provides mechanisms of compliance. The authority is sustained by an institutional framework that existed before the judge's appointment and will persist after it.

The authority of a military officer over soldiers derives from the institutional structure of the armed forces — the officer holds a commission conferred through established procedures, gives orders within a defined chain of command, and can enforce compliance through military discipline. The authority is sustained by the institution of the military, not by the officer's personal qualities.

The authority of a university dean over faculty members derives from the institutional structure of the university — the dean is appointed through a faculty process, exercises powers defined by the university charter, and makes decisions within established governance procedures. The authority is sustained by the institution, not by the individual.
definition: A person P has authority over a group G if and only if (i) P occupies a position within an institutional structure that confers the right to issue directives, (ii) the members of G recognise P's right to issue directives, and (iii) P's directives are enforceable through institutional mechanisms.
conditions: P occupies an institutional position conferring directive rights
G recognises P's right to issue directives
P's directives are enforceable through institutional mechanisms
counterexample: Consider a charismatic religious leader who has no institutional position — no church has ordained or appointed him, no established body confers his status. Yet thousands of followers defer to his pronouncements on matters of conduct and belief. They recognise his authority not because of any institutional appointment but because of the force of his personality, the apparent depth of his insight, and the transformative effect of his teachings upon their lives. His directives are not enforceable through any institutional mechanism — there are no sanctions for disobedience beyond the withdrawal of his personal attention. Yet his authority over his followers is profound and undeniable.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a common structure: in each, the authority of the individual is derived from and sustained by an institutional framework that exists independently of the individual's personal qualities. The judge's authority comes from the legal system; the officer's from the military; the dean's from the university. The charismatic leader's authority derives not from any institution but from the personal qualities he exhibits and the direct effect of his presence upon his followers. The definition captures institutional authority — the most formalised and common variety — but omits the phenomenon of charismatic authority, in which the leader's personal qualities generate deference and obedience without the mediation of any institution. The paradigmatic cases reveal the typical structure of authority in organised societies, but the charismatic case shows that authority can arise from personal qualities alone when those qualities inspire sufficient devotion.
passage:
The most familiar forms of authority in organised societies share a common structure: they are derived from and sustained by institutional frameworks. A judge exercises authority because a legal system confers it — the judge is appointed through established procedures, renders decisions within a defined jurisdiction, and can enforce compliance through the mechanisms of law. A military officer exercises authority because the armed forces confer it — the officer holds a commission within a chain of command and can enforce obedience through military discipline. A university dean exercises authority because the university confers it — the dean is appointed through faculty governance and makes decisions within a charter. In each case, the individual's authority is institutional in origin and survives the departure of the particular occupant.

One might attempt to characterise authority as requiring an institutional position that confers directive rights, recognition by the governed, and enforceability through institutional mechanisms. This definition captures the standard cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the phenomenon of charismatic leadership. Consider a religious teacher who holds no institutional position — no church has ordained him, no body has conferred his status, no charter defines his powers. Yet thousands of followers defer to his pronouncements on matters of conduct, alter their behaviour in accordance with his teachings, and regard his judgement as binding. His authority is not enforceable through any institutional mechanism; there are no sanctions for disobedience beyond the withdrawal of his personal regard. Yet his authority over his followers is as real as any judge's authority over litigants. What the background cases possess and the charismatic case lacks is institutional mediation — the framework of appointment, charter, and enforcement that sustains authority independently of the individual's personal qualities. The definition, by requiring institutional structure, excludes a form of authority that is ancient, powerful, and ubiquitous in human affairs.

background_cases: When two neighbouring farmers agree to share the labour of harvesting each other's fields, each contributing a day's work in exchange for a day's help, the arrangement is cooperative. Each farmer contributes voluntarily, each benefits from the other's labour, and the arrangement is sustained by mutual understanding and reciprocal contribution.

When the members of a string quartet rehearse and perform together, each contributing a distinct musical part, the activity is cooperative. Each musician adjusts to the others in real time, each contributes a specialised skill, and the result — the performance — is a joint achievement that no individual could produce alone.

When the crew of a lifeboat rows together to reach a sinking vessel, each pulling an oar in coordination with the others, the activity is cooperative. Each crew member contributes effort, each adjusts to the rhythm of the others, and the goal — reaching the vessel — can be achieved only through their combined and coordinated action.
definition: A group of persons is engaged in cooperation if and only if (i) each member voluntarily contributes effort towards a shared goal, (ii) each member's contribution is coordinated with the contributions of others, and (iii) each member acts with the intention of achieving the shared goal.
conditions: Each member voluntarily contributes effort towards a shared goal
Each member's contribution is coordinated with others
Each member acts with the intention of achieving the shared goal
counterexample: Consider a busy urban intersection where traffic flows smoothly without traffic lights or a police officer. Each driver adjusts speed and direction in response to the movements of other drivers, yielding, merging, and signalling. The result is an orderly flow of traffic — a shared goal, one might say, since all drivers wish to pass through the intersection safely. Each driver contributes effort and coordinates with others. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet no driver intends to cooperate with the others; each is simply trying to get through the intersection without collision. The orderly flow is an unintended emergent result of many individually self-interested decisions, not a joint achievement produced by cooperative intention.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the traffic case: in each, the participants are aware of one another as cooperators and deliberately adjust their actions for the sake of the shared enterprise. The farmers agree to help one another. The quartet members rehearse together and listen to one another. The lifeboat crew pull in unison for a common purpose. In the traffic case, each driver adjusts to others only to avoid collision, not for the sake of a shared project. The orderly flow is a by-product of individual prudence rather than a product of cooperative intention. The definition captures the coordination and shared-goal features of cooperation but cannot distinguish genuine cooperation — in which participants deliberately act together — from emergent order produced by the coincidence of individually self-interested actions.
passage:
The activities most clearly recognised as cooperation share a feature that distinguishes them from other forms of coordinated behaviour. When two farmers agree to share the labour of harvest, each is aware of the other as a partner in a joint enterprise and adjusts his contribution accordingly. When the members of a string quartet perform, each musician listens to the others and adapts in real time, aware that the performance depends upon their joint responsiveness. When a lifeboat crew rows towards a sinking vessel, each crew member pulls in time with the others, aware that only their combined effort can achieve the goal. In each case, the participants are conscious of their mutual dependence and deliberately coordinate for the sake of a shared purpose.

One might attempt to characterise cooperation as a relation among persons who voluntarily contribute effort towards a shared goal, coordinate their contributions, and act with the intention of achieving that goal. This definition captures the standard cases well. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the phenomenon of spontaneous order. Consider a busy urban intersection without traffic lights or a directing officer. Each driver adjusts speed and direction in response to the movements of other drivers — yielding here, merging there, signalling intentions. The result is an orderly flow of traffic, and since all drivers wish to pass through safely, one might say they share a goal. Each contributes effort and coordinates with others. All three conditions appear met. Yet no driver intends to cooperate; each is simply trying to get through without collision. The orderly flow is an unintended emergent result of many individually prudent decisions. What the background cases possess and the traffic case lacks is the participants' awareness of one another as cooperators in a joint enterprise. The definition attends to coordination and shared goals but cannot distinguish the deliberate joint action of cooperators from the coincidental coordination of agents who happen to pursue compatible individual aims.

background_cases: When a judge issues a ruling in a court of law, the ruling carries authority because the judge holds a position conferred through an established legal process — appointment, examination, or election — and the process by which the judge came to hold the position is itself governed by rules that the community regards as fair and legitimate.

When a captain gives orders aboard a ship, the orders carry authority because the captain's position is conferred by a recognised process — licensing, examination, and appointment by the ship's owners — and the crew's obedience rests on their recognition that the captain's competence and position were established through trustworthy procedures.

When a headmaster sets rules for a school, the rules carry authority because the headmaster's appointment was made through a process — selection by a governing board, subject to public accountability — that the school community regards as proper and well-ordered.
definition: A person P has authority over a group G with respect to a domain D if and only if (i) P occupies a recognised position within an institution governing D, (ii) P issues directives concerning D, and (iii) members of G generally comply with P's directives.
conditions: P occupies a recognised position within an institution governing D
P issues directives concerning D
Members of G generally comply with P's directives
counterexample: Suppose a man arrives at a village and, through a combination of personal charisma and strategic gift-giving, comes to occupy the recognised position of village headman within the informal institution of village governance. He issues directives — settle disputes this way, allocate common land that way — and the villagers generally comply. All three conditions are met. Yet the man's position was gained through manipulation and patronage rather than through any process the villagers would, upon reflection, regard as fair. His authority is of a debased kind — closer to domination than to genuine authority.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the person's position was conferred through a process that the group would, upon reflection, regard as fair and well-ordered — a process governed by rules of competence, accountability, and procedural propriety. The definition fails to require that the process by which P came to occupy the position be one the group regards as legitimate, rather than one achieved through manipulation, patronage, or force.
passage:
When we examine paradigmatic cases of authority — a judge ruling in a court, a captain commanding a ship, a headmaster governing a school — a common feature emerges regarding the process by which the position was attained. The judge was appointed through an established legal process. The captain earned his position through examination and licensing. The headmaster was selected by a governing board subject to public accountability. In each case, the authority of the position rests upon the legitimacy of the process by which it was conferred.

One might attempt to characterise authority by stipulating that a person has authority over a group with respect to a domain if he occupies a recognised position within an institution governing that domain, issues directives, and enjoys general compliance. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from village life. Suppose a man arrives in a small village and, through a combination of personal charisma and strategic gift-giving to influential families, comes to occupy the recognised position of headman within the village's informal institution of governance. He issues directives concerning the allocation of common land and the resolution of disputes, and the villagers generally comply — partly from genuine respect, partly from a sense of obligation created by his gifts. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet one might observe that the man's authority is of a debased kind. His position was gained not through any process the villagers would, upon reflection, regard as fair, but through manipulation and the creation of personal dependence. What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the process by which the person came to occupy the position be one the group regards as procedurally fair and well-ordered — governed by standards of competence, accountability, and propriety.

background_cases: When a teacher distributes marks according to the quality of pupils' essays, the distribution is just not merely because it follows a known rule but because the rule tracks the relevant consideration — the merit of the work — and thereby gives each pupil what she is due in virtue of her performance.

When a hospital allocates scarce organs for transplantation according to medical urgency and likelihood of success, the allocation is just because the criteria track the relevant considerations — the severity of need and the probability of benefit — rather than irrelevant factors such as wealth or social standing.

When a court awards damages proportional to the harm suffered by the plaintiff, the award is just because the criterion — the magnitude of the harm — is the consideration relevant to rectifying the wrong, and the award gives the injured party what she is due in virtue of her loss.
definition: A distribution D of goods or burdens among members of a group G is just if and only if (i) D follows a rule that is publicly known, (ii) the rule is applied consistently to all members of G, and (iii) no member of G is arbitrarily excluded from D.
conditions: D follows a rule that is publicly known
The rule is applied consistently to all members of G
No member of G is arbitrarily excluded from D
counterexample: Suppose a village distributes its harvest according to a publicly known rule that assigns each household a share proportional to the height of the head of household. The rule is consistently applied. No one is excluded. All three conditions are met. Yet the distribution is plainly unjust, for the height of the head of household bears no relevant relation to need, contribution, or desert. A short widow raising five children receives less than a tall bachelor living alone.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the distributing rule tracks a consideration that is genuinely relevant to the good being distributed — merit for academic marks, medical need for organs, harm suffered for damages. The definition fails to require that the distributing rule be substantively appropriate — that it track considerations relevant to the type of good or burden in question, rather than merely being public, consistent, and inclusive.
passage:
When we consider paradigmatic instances of just distribution, a common feature emerges concerning the basis of the distributing rule. The teacher who assigns marks according to the quality of work is just because the criterion tracks the relevant consideration — the merit of the essay. The hospital that allocates organs according to medical urgency and likelihood of success is just because the criteria are relevant to the purpose of the distribution. The court that awards damages proportional to the harm suffered is just because the magnitude of the loss is the pertinent consideration. In each case, the distributing rule is not merely public and consistent but substantively appropriate — it tracks a consideration that bears a genuine relation to the good being distributed.

One might attempt to characterise justice in distribution by stipulating that a distribution is just if it follows a publicly known rule, applied consistently, with no member arbitrarily excluded. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the life of a small community. Suppose a village distributes its harvest according to a publicly known rule that assigns each household a share proportional to the height of the head of household. The rule is applied consistently to every family. No one is arbitrarily excluded. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the distribution is manifestly unjust. A short widow raising five children receives a meagre portion, whilst a tall bachelor living alone receives an abundance. The height of the head of household bears no relevant relation to need, contribution, or any other consideration pertinent to the distribution of food. What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the distributing rule track a consideration genuinely relevant to the good in question — that it be substantively appropriate, not merely procedurally correct.

background_cases: The norm against theft in most human communities is sustained not merely by the threat of punishment but by a widely shared conviction that taking what belongs to another is wrong — that persons have a right to the fruits of their labour, and that to violate this right is to act unjustly. The norm's binding force derives from this moral conviction.

The norm of truthfulness in ordinary discourse is sustained not merely by the practical inconvenience of being caught in falsehood but by a widely shared conviction that deliberate deception is a kind of injury — that persons are owed honesty in their dealings, and that to deceive is to treat another as a mere instrument. The norm draws its authority from this moral commitment.

The norm of parental care — that parents ought to provide for their children — is sustained not merely by social pressure but by a widely shared conviction that children are helpless beings with a claim upon the adults who brought them into existence, and that to neglect a child is to fail in a profound moral duty.
definition: A regularity of behaviour R constitutes a genuine social norm for a group G if and only if (i) the majority of members of G conform to R, (ii) members of G expect conformity from one another, and (iii) deviation from R is met with some form of social sanction.
conditions: The majority of members of G conform to R
Members of G expect conformity from one another
Deviation from R is met with some form of social sanction
counterexample: Consider the regularity, observed in many British offices, of making a round of tea for one's colleagues when one makes tea for oneself. The majority conform. Each person expects conformity — an office worker who makes tea only for herself attracts notice. And deviation is met with mild social sanction — disapproving remarks, pointed jokes. All three conditions are met. Yet the tea-round convention, though a genuine regularity sustained by social pressure, does not possess the moral weight of a norm against theft or deception. It is a custom, enforced by habit and mild disapproval, not a norm grounded in a shared moral conviction.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the regularity is sustained by a widely shared conviction that conformity is morally required — that deviation constitutes not merely a breach of custom but a moral failing. The definition fails to require that the expectation of conformity and the sanction for deviation be grounded in a shared moral conviction, and thereby cannot distinguish genuine norms from mere conventions maintained by habit and social pressure.
passage:
When we examine the most robust social norms — the prohibition of theft, the expectation of truthfulness, the duty of parental care — a common feature of their normative force emerges. Each is sustained not merely by conformity, expectation, and sanction but by a widely shared moral conviction. The prohibition of theft rests on the conviction that persons have a right to the fruits of their labour. The expectation of truthfulness rests on the conviction that deliberate deception is a species of injury. The duty of parental care rests on the conviction that children have a claim upon those who brought them into existence. In each case, the norm draws its binding force from a shared moral commitment that goes beyond mere custom.

One might attempt to characterise a genuine social norm as a regularity to which the majority conform, from which conformity is expected, and from which deviation is met with social sanction. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the customs of the British office. In many workplaces, it is the practice to make a round of tea for one's colleagues whenever one makes tea for oneself. The majority conform to this practice. Each worker expects the others to observe it. And a worker who persistently makes tea only for herself is met with disapproval — pointed remarks, mild ostracism. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the tea-round convention, for all its social force, is not a genuine norm in the fullest sense. It lacks the moral weight of the prohibition of theft or the expectation of truthfulness. It is a custom, sustained by habit and mild social pressure, not a norm grounded in a shared conviction about what morality requires. What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the expectation of conformity be rooted in a widely shared moral conviction — that deviation be regarded not merely as a breach of etiquette but as a moral failing.

background_cases: A small fishing village in which families have lived together for generations, sharing the harbour, the school, and the church, constitutes a community: its members know one another by name, participate in common activities, and share a sense of belonging rooted in geographical proximity and shared daily life.

A traditional farming settlement in which households cooperate in planting and harvest, share water rights, and celebrate seasonal festivals together constitutes a community: its members interact daily, share resources, and identify with the settlement as their home.

A residential neighbourhood in a small town whose members share parks, schools, and local shops, attend the same houses of worship, and participate in neighbourhood associations constitutes a community: proximity, regular interaction, and shared identity bind the members together.
definition: A group G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G live in close geographical proximity, (ii) the members interact face-to-face on a regular basis, and (iii) the members share a sense of mutual obligation rooted in their co-residence.
conditions: The members of G live in close geographical proximity
The members interact face-to-face on a regular basis
The members share a sense of mutual obligation rooted in their co-residence
counterexample: Consider a community of scholars working in a specialised field of ancient history, scattered across universities on different continents. They do not live in proximity; they may never have met face-to-face. Yet they read one another's work, correspond extensively, attend the same conferences when possible, uphold shared standards of evidence and argumentation, and feel a strong mutual obligation to maintain the integrity of their discipline. They describe themselves as a community, and the description is apt. The first two conditions are violated, yet the community is real.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that community is mediated by geographical proximity and face-to-face interaction, but the definition mistakes these historically common vehicles of community for necessary conditions, overlooking communities constituted by shared intellectual purposes, professional standards, and mutual obligations that operate independently of place.
passage:
The fishing village, the farming settlement, and the residential neighbourhood each constitute a community in which geographical proximity, face-to-face interaction, and a sense of mutual obligation grounded in co-residence bind the members together. These cases suggest a definition: a community is a group whose members live near one another, interact regularly in person, and share obligations arising from their shared location.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this geographically grounded account may be drawn from the world of scholarship. Consider a community of scholars working in a specialised field of ancient history — the decipherment of Linear B, perhaps, or the archaeology of Mesopotamia. Its members are scattered across universities on three or four continents. Many have never met face-to-face; their interaction is conducted through published articles, letters, and the occasional conference. They do not live in proximity; they share no neighbourhood, no school, no harbour. Yet they constitute a community in a perfectly genuine sense. They read and respond to one another's work with care. They uphold shared standards of evidence and argumentation. They feel a mutual obligation to maintain the integrity and progress of their discipline. They describe themselves as a community, and the description is universally accepted. It appears that the background cases, drawn from traditional settlements, share the feature of geographical proximity, and that the definition has mistaken this common but contingent feature for a necessary condition of community as such.

background_cases: The Parliament of the United Kingdom is an institution: it was formally established, operates according to written rules and procedures, serves the recognised function of legislation, and has a defined membership and structure.

The University of Oxford is an institution: it was formally founded, operates according to statutes and regulations, serves the recognised function of education and research, and has a defined organisational structure with faculties, colleges, and officers.

The Bank of England is an institution: it was formally established by charter, operates according to regulatory frameworks, serves the recognised function of monetary policy and financial stability, and has a defined organisational hierarchy.
definition: A social arrangement S is an institution if and only if (i) S was deliberately created for a specific purpose, (ii) S has a formal organisational structure with defined roles and procedures, and (iii) S is recognised by the wider society as having authority within its domain.
conditions: S was deliberately created for a specific purpose
S has a formal organisational structure with defined roles and procedures
S is recognised by the wider society as having authority within its domain
counterexample: Consider the institution of private property. It is among the most fundamental institutions of social life — it structures economic relations, underpins legal systems, and shapes the distribution of wealth. It is recognised by society as having authority in its domain. Yet it was not deliberately created for a specific purpose by any identifiable founder. It has no formal organisational structure with defined roles and procedures in the way that a parliament or university does. It emerged gradually through custom, practice, and the accumulation of legal precedent over centuries. The first two conditions are violated, yet private property is an institution of the first importance.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that they are formal organisations — deliberately founded bodies with explicit structures and charters. The definition mistakes the features of formal organisations for necessary conditions of institutions as such, overlooking the vast class of informal institutions — property, marriage, money, language — that arise spontaneously through social practice and have no formal structure or identifiable moment of creation.
passage:
Parliament, the University of Oxford, and the Bank of England are paradigmatic institutions: each was deliberately created, possesses a formal organisational structure, and is recognised by society as authoritative within its domain. These cases suggest a definition: an institution is a deliberately created social arrangement with a formal structure and recognised authority.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the most fundamental institutions of social life. Consider the institution of private property. It is among the most important institutions known to any society — it structures economic relations, underpins legal systems, and shapes the distribution of resources and opportunities. Society recognises its authority: legal systems enforce property rights, and social norms protect possessions. Yet private property was not deliberately created for a specific purpose by any identifiable founder or founding body. It has no formal organisational structure with defined roles and procedures in the way that a parliament or university does. It emerged gradually, over centuries, through custom, practice, dispute resolution, and the slow accumulation of legal precedent. The first two conditions are violated. It appears that the background cases are all formal organisations — bodies deliberately founded and explicitly structured — and that the definition has mistaken the features of formal organisations for the necessary conditions of institutions as such, thereby excluding the informal institutions — property, marriage, money, language — that are arguably more fundamental than any formal body.

background_cases: The norm against stealing is a social norm: it prescribes behaviour (do not take what belongs to another), is widely followed, and violation is met with punishment — legal sanction, social censure, or both. The norm is enforced by a combination of internalised moral feeling and external institutional mechanisms.

The norm of truth-telling in commercial transactions is a social norm: it prescribes that merchants should not deceive their customers, is widely followed, and violation may result in legal liability, loss of reputation, or social ostracism. The norm is sustained by both internal conscience and external enforcement.

The norm of reciprocity — returning favours, repaying debts, honouring obligations — is a social norm: it prescribes a pattern of behaviour, is widely followed, and violation may result in the loss of trust and social standing. The norm is enforced by the prospect of social consequences.
definition: A rule R is a social norm if and only if (i) R prescribes or proscribes a type of behaviour, (ii) R is sustained by the threat of sanctions for non-compliance, and (iii) R serves the collective interest of the group in which it operates.
conditions: R prescribes or proscribes a type of behaviour
R is sustained by the threat of sanctions for non-compliance
R serves the collective interest of the group
counterexample: Consider the norm in many societies that requires a bride's family to provide a dowry to the groom's family upon marriage. This norm prescribes behaviour and is sustained by the threat of social sanctions — families that fail to provide an adequate dowry may face ostracism or the dissolution of the marriage arrangement. Yet it is far from clear that the norm serves the collective interest of the group. It may impoverish the bride's family, reinforce economic inequality between families, and place a disproportionate burden upon those with daughters. The norm persists despite its failure to serve the collective interest — it is sustained by tradition, social pressure, and the fear of sanction.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the norms in question plausibly serve the collective interest — honesty, property security, and reciprocity benefit the group as a whole. The definition mistakes this feature of beneficial norms for a necessary condition of all social norms, overlooking norms that persist through tradition and coercion despite being harmful, unjust, or contrary to the collective interest.
passage:
The norms against stealing, of commercial truth-telling, and of reciprocity each prescribe behaviour, are sustained by sanctions, and plausibly serve the collective interest: a society in which property is secure, merchants are honest, and debts are repaid is better off than one in which these norms do not hold. These cases suggest a functional definition: a social norm is a sanctioned rule of behaviour that serves the collective interest.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the functional requirement may be drawn from the practice of dowry. In many societies, the norm requires the bride's family to provide a substantial dowry to the groom's family upon marriage. The norm prescribes behaviour — a specific transfer of wealth. It is sustained by the threat of sanctions: families that fail to provide an adequate dowry may face social ostracism, the breaking of the engagement, or worse. But it is far from clear that the norm serves the collective interest of the group. It may impoverish the bride's family, entrench economic inequality, and impose a disproportionate burden upon those with many daughters. The norm persists not because it benefits the group but because tradition, social pressure, and the fear of sanction sustain it. It appears that the background cases are instances of beneficial norms, and that the definition has mistaken the beneficence of those particular norms for a necessary feature of normativity as such. Social norms, one might observe, may persist and function as norms even when they are harmful, unjust, or contrary to the interests of the group in which they operate.

background_cases: When a patient trusts her physician, the trust derives its force not merely from a prediction that the physician will act competently but from the patient's belief that the physician has accepted a role carrying obligations — that the physician regards herself as bound by a professional duty of care, so that the patient's vulnerability is met not by mere benevolence but by a recognised normative commitment.

When citizens trust the judiciary, the trust rests upon the belief that judges are bound by the obligations of their office — that they will decide cases according to law not merely because it is in their interest to do so but because they recognise the normative authority of the judicial role and regard themselves as committed to its demands.

When a depositor trusts a bank, the trust is grounded in the belief that the bank operates within a framework of regulatory obligations and fiduciary duties — that it will safeguard the depositor's funds not merely from self-interest but because it is bound by norms that attach to its institutional role.
definition: Agent A trusts agent B if and only if (i) A expects that B will act in a manner favourable to A's interests, (ii) A is willing to be vulnerable to B's actions, and (iii) A's expectation is based upon B's perceived competence.
conditions: A expects that B will act in a manner favourable to A's interests
A is willing to be vulnerable to B's actions
A's expectation is based upon B's perceived competence
counterexample: Consider a patient who trusts a newly qualified physician whom she has every reason to regard as barely competent — she has been told that the physician graduated last in his class and has no clinical experience. Nevertheless, she trusts him because she believes that as a physician he is bound by a professional oath and by the norms of medical practice, and that he will exert himself to fulfil these obligations even if his skills are limited. She expects favourable action, and she makes herself vulnerable.
