But her trust is grounded not in perceived competence but in the belief that the physician regards himself as normatively committed to her care. The third condition is not met, yet the patient's attitude is plainly one of trust.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that trust is grounded in the belief that the trusted party recognises and accepts a normative commitment — a duty, obligation, or role-based responsibility — that goes beyond mere competence or self-interest. The definition, by grounding trust in perceived competence, cannot account for cases in which trust is extended despite doubts about competence, on the basis of the trusted party's perceived commitment to the obligations of a role.
passage:
The concept of trust plays a central role in social philosophy, and certain paradigmatic cases illuminate the structural features essential to its analysis. When a patient trusts her physician, the trust derives its force from the belief that the physician has accepted a role carrying obligations — that she regards herself as bound by a professional duty of care. When citizens trust the judiciary, the trust rests upon the belief that judges recognise the normative authority of their office and regard themselves as committed to its demands. When a depositor trusts a bank, the trust is grounded in the belief that the institution operates within a framework of fiduciary duties and regulatory obligations.

These cases invite a general characterisation: trust obtains when one agent expects another to act favourably, is willing to be vulnerable, and bases this expectation upon the other's perceived competence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from medical practice. Suppose a patient is treated by a newly qualified physician whom she has every reason to regard as barely competent. She has been informed that he graduated at the bottom of his class and has virtually no clinical experience. She entertains serious doubts about his technical ability. Nevertheless, she trusts him. Her trust is grounded not in any perception of competence but in the belief that, as a physician, he is bound by a professional oath and by the norms of medical practice — that he will strive to fulfil the obligations of his role even if his skills are limited. She expects that he will act in her interest, and she makes herself vulnerable to his care. The third condition of the proposed definition is not satisfied, since her expectation rests not on competence but on normative commitment. Yet her attitude is plainly one of trust. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that trust be grounded in the belief that the trusted party recognises and accepts a normative commitment that goes beyond mere ability.

background_cases: When two farmers agree to help each other with their respective harvests — each contributing labour to the other's fields in turn — the cooperation is genuine because each party adjusts his behaviour in the light of the other's needs and contributions, and each accepts a constraint upon his own immediate interests for the sake of a jointly beneficial outcome that neither could achieve alone.

When members of a string quartet perform a piece of music together, the cooperation consists not merely in their simultaneous playing but in the continuous mutual adjustment of tempo, dynamics, and expression — each musician listens to the others and modifies her own playing in response, subordinating her individual interpretation to the demands of the collective performance.

When two scientists collaborate on a research project, each contributing different expertise, the cooperation is constituted by the mutual adjustment of their respective contributions — the theorist frames hypotheses in terms the experimentalist can test, and the experimentalist designs procedures that address the theorist's questions, each shaping his work in the light of the other's.
definition: An interaction between agents A and B constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) A and B act toward a common goal, (ii) their actions are mutually beneficial, and (iii) both A and B are aware that they are acting toward the same goal.
conditions: A and B act toward a common goal
Their actions are mutually beneficial
Both A and B are aware that they are acting toward the same goal
counterexample: Consider two rival shopkeepers on the same street, each of whom advertises aggressively to attract customers. The advertising of each draws foot traffic to the street, benefiting both — a customer drawn by one shop's advertisement often visits the other shop as well. Both shopkeepers act toward the common goal of increasing foot traffic on the street. Their actions are mutually beneficial. And both are aware that their advertising serves this common purpose. All three conditions are met. Yet the shopkeepers are not cooperating; they are competing, each striving to capture customers at the expense of the other, and the mutual benefit is an unintended by-product of their rivalry rather than the object of a coordinated effort.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that cooperation involves the deliberate mutual adjustment of actions — each party shapes her contribution in the light of the other's, accepting constraints upon her own immediate interests for the sake of a jointly pursued outcome. The definition, by requiring only a common goal, mutual benefit, and mutual awareness, cannot distinguish genuine cooperation from competitive interactions that happen to produce mutual benefits as an unintended consequence.
passage:
The concept of cooperation occupies a central place in social philosophy, and certain cases illuminate the structural features essential to its analysis. When two farmers agree to help each other with their harvests, each contributing labour to the other's fields, the cooperation consists in the mutual adjustment of effort — each accepts a constraint upon his immediate interests for the sake of a jointly beneficial outcome. When members of a string quartet perform together, the cooperation lies in the continuous mutual accommodation of tempo, dynamics, and expression, each player modifying her own performance in response to the others. When two scientists collaborate, each shapes his contribution in the light of the other's expertise, subordinating his individual approach to the demands of the shared investigation.

These cases invite a general characterisation: cooperation obtains when agents act toward a common goal, their actions are mutually beneficial, and both are aware that they pursue the same end. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from commercial life. Consider two rival shopkeepers on the same street, each of whom advertises aggressively to attract customers. The advertising of each draws foot traffic to the street, and a customer attracted by one shop's advertisement frequently visits the neighbouring establishment as well. Both shopkeepers act toward the common goal of increasing the number of visitors to the street. Their actions are mutually beneficial — each profits from the other's advertising. And both are aware of this shared benefit. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the shopkeepers are manifestly not cooperating. They are competing, each striving to capture customers at the expense of the other, and the mutual benefit that results is an unintended by-product of their rivalry rather than the object of any coordinated effort. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is the deliberate mutual adjustment of actions, each party shaping her contribution in the light of the other's, a feature entirely absent from the competitive shopkeepers' interaction.

background_cases: When the common law tradition transmits legal principles from one generation of judges to the next, the tradition's vitality depends upon the fact that each generation does not merely replicate the rulings of its predecessors but actively interprets, adapts, and refines them in the light of new circumstances — the tradition persists precisely because it is a living process of interpretive engagement rather than a mechanical repetition of fixed rules.

When a craft tradition — such as the making of pottery in a particular region — is transmitted from master to apprentice, the tradition endures not through the exact replication of forms and techniques but through the apprentice's creative appropriation of the master's methods, adapting them to new materials, new tools, and new aesthetic sensibilities while preserving the recognisable character of the tradition.

When a musical tradition is passed from one generation of performers to the next, the tradition lives because each generation interprets the inherited repertoire in new ways — introducing variations in tempo, ornamentation, and expressive emphasis — while maintaining the recognisable identity of the tradition through fidelity to its central themes and forms.
definition: A practice P constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted from one generation to the next, (ii) P remains substantially unchanged across generations, and (iii) the practitioners of P regard it as an inherited practice.
conditions: P has been transmitted from one generation to the next
P remains substantially unchanged across generations
The practitioners of P regard it as an inherited practice
counterexample: Consider the tradition of English portrait painting, which has been transmitted from the Tudor era through the Enlightenment and into the modern period. The practice has been transmitted across many generations, and its practitioners regard it as an inherited tradition. Yet the tradition has not remained substantially unchanged: the stiff, hieratic poses of the Elizabethan era gave way to the psychological depth of Reynolds, the romantic grandeur of Lawrence, and the modernist experiments of Freud. In each period, the tradition was so thoroughly transformed that a Tudor portrait and a twentieth-century portrait share little in terms of technique, style, or philosophical approach to the sitter. The second condition is not met, yet the practice is unquestionably a tradition.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that a tradition is constituted not by the invariance of a practice across generations but by a process of active interpretive engagement — each generation receives, adapts, and transforms the inherited practice while maintaining a recognisable continuity of identity. The definition, by requiring that the practice remain substantially unchanged, mistakes stasis for tradition and cannot accommodate the creative transformation that is essential to the vitality of any living tradition.
passage:
The concept of tradition has been the subject of sustained philosophical analysis, and certain cases illuminate the features essential to any adequate account. When the common law tradition transmits legal principles from one generation of judges to the next, its vitality depends upon the active interpretive engagement of each generation — judges do not merely replicate the rulings of their predecessors but adapt and refine them in the light of new circumstances. When a pottery tradition is transmitted from master to apprentice, the apprentice does not merely copy the master's forms but creatively appropriates his methods, adapting them to new materials and sensibilities. When a musical tradition passes from one generation of performers to the next, each generation introduces new interpretive emphases while preserving the recognisable character of the inherited repertoire.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a tradition is a practice transmitted from generation to generation, remaining substantially unchanged, and regarded by its practitioners as inherited. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the history of English portrait painting. The tradition of English portraiture has been transmitted across many generations, from the Tudor era through the Enlightenment and into the modern period. Its practitioners have consistently regarded it as an inherited tradition, situating their own work within a lineage stretching back centuries. Yet the tradition has not remained substantially unchanged. The stiff, hieratic compositions of the Elizabethan period bear little resemblance to the psychological depth of Reynolds, the romantic grandeur of Lawrence, or the unflinching modernism of Lucian Freud. In each period, the tradition was so thoroughly transformed that works from different centuries share little in terms of technique, composition, or approach to the human subject. The second condition of the proposed definition is not met. Yet English portrait painting is unquestionably a tradition. One might observe that what the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to capture — is that a tradition is constituted not by the invariance of a practice but by a continuous process of interpretive engagement in which each generation transforms the inherited material while maintaining a recognisable continuity of identity.

background_cases: A villager entrusts his savings to a neighbour who has faithfully managed communal funds for twenty years. The neighbour's long record of honest dealing provides the basis for the villager's confidence, and the trust is well placed. The villager makes himself vulnerable — his savings could be lost — but the neighbour's established track record grounds his willingness to accept this risk.

A patient trusts her physician on the basis of the physician's long history of successful treatment and honest communication. The physician has repeatedly demonstrated competence and integrity, and the patient's trust is grounded in this accumulated evidence. The patient makes herself vulnerable by following the physician's recommendations, but the physician's record justifies this vulnerability.

A business partner trusts his associate on the basis of five years of profitable and transparent collaboration. The associate has consistently honoured agreements, disclosed relevant information, and acted in the partnership's interest. The trusting partner makes himself vulnerable by committing resources, but the associate's demonstrated reliability grounds his confidence.
definition: Agent A trusts agent B with respect to a matter M if and only if (i) A is willing to make themselves vulnerable to B's actions regarding M, (ii) A's willingness is grounded in evidence of B's past reliable behaviour, and (iii) B has a track record of acting competently and honestly with respect to matters relevantly similar to M.
conditions: A is willing to make themselves vulnerable to B's actions regarding M
A's willingness is grounded in evidence of B's past reliable behaviour
B has a track record of acting competently and honestly with respect to matters relevantly similar to M
counterexample: Suppose an infant trusts her mother to provide nourishment and comfort. The infant is willing to make herself vulnerable — indeed, she has no alternative, being entirely dependent. Yet the infant's willingness is not grounded in evidence of past reliable behaviour, for the infant lacks the cognitive capacity to assess evidence or to form beliefs about her mother's track record. The trust is immediate, unreflective, and grounded in biological attachment rather than in any assessment of the mother's history. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are not satisfied in any meaningful sense, and yet the trust between infant and mother is perhaps the most fundamental form of trust in human life.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that trust is a reflective, evidence-based attitude adopted by a cognitively mature agent who has assessed the trustee's track record. The definition captures this structure faithfully. Yet the case of infant trust reveals that the most basic and pervasive form of trust in human life is pre-reflective, arising from biological attachment and dependency rather than from the assessment of evidence. The inductive generalisation from cases of mature, evidence-based trust fails to encompass the foundational stratum of trust upon which all later, reflective trust is built.
passage:
The most instructive cases of trust in social life share a common structure. A villager entrusts his savings to a neighbour whose twenty years of honest fund management provide ample grounds for confidence. A patient trusts her physician on the basis of a long record of competent treatment. A business partner trusts his associate after five years of transparent and profitable collaboration. In each case, the trusting agent makes herself vulnerable, her willingness is grounded in evidence of past reliable behaviour, and the trusted party has a demonstrated track record of competence and honesty.

These cases invite the generalisation that trust consists in a willingness to be vulnerable, grounded in evidence of past reliability, directed toward an agent with a demonstrated track record. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the earliest stages of human life. Suppose an infant trusts her mother to provide nourishment and comfort. The infant is willing to be vulnerable — indeed, she has no choice, being wholly dependent. Yet her willingness is not grounded in any assessment of evidence, for she lacks the cognitive capacity to evaluate her mother's past behaviour or to form beliefs about reliability. The trust is immediate, unreflective, and rooted in biological attachment rather than in evidential reasoning. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are not satisfied in any meaningful sense. Yet one might observe that the trust between infant and mother is perhaps the most fundamental form of trust in human existence — the foundation upon which all later, reflective trust is built. What the background cases share is the reflective, evidence-based character of mature trust. The generalisation from these cases fails to encompass the pre-reflective, biologically grounded stratum of trust that precedes and underlies all evidential assessment.

background_cases: Two farmers agree to help each other harvest their fields in alternation: one week the first farmer's crop is gathered by both, the next week the second farmer's crop. Each farmer contributes labour, each benefits from the arrangement, and both understand themselves to be working together toward a shared purpose. The cooperation is voluntary, reciprocal, and mutually understood.

A group of neighbours organise a rota for clearing snow from the shared lane. Each household takes a turn, contributing labour that benefits all. The arrangement is voluntary, each participant understands the shared purpose, and the contributions are reciprocal over time. This is a clear instance of cooperation.

Two scientists collaborate on a research paper, each contributing expertise in a different area. The collaboration is voluntary, each understands the shared goal of publication, and each benefits from the other's contribution. The cooperation is deliberate and mutually acknowledged.
definition: An interaction among agents constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) the agents voluntarily coordinate their actions toward a shared goal, (ii) each agent contributes something — labour, resources, or expertise — that the others lack, and (iii) each agent benefits from the interaction.
conditions: The agents voluntarily coordinate their actions toward a shared goal
Each agent contributes something that the others lack
Each agent benefits from the interaction
counterexample: Suppose a powerful landlord offers starving tenants the following arrangement: they will work his fields collectively, pooling their labour toward the shared goal of bringing in the harvest, and in return each will receive a portion of the grain sufficient to survive the winter. The tenants coordinate their actions toward the shared goal of harvesting; each contributes labour that the landlord, being one person, cannot supply alone; and each benefits, since without the grain they would starve. All three conditions are met. Yet the tenants' participation is not genuinely voluntary — they cooperate because the alternative is starvation, and the terms of the arrangement are dictated entirely by the landlord. One might hesitate to describe this as cooperation in the full sense; it is closer to coerced collective labour under the guise of mutual benefit.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the agents enter the cooperative arrangement from positions of rough equality and genuine freedom of choice. The definition, generalised from these cases, treats voluntariness as a binary condition — either the agents choose to participate or they do not. Yet the case of the starving tenants reveals that voluntariness admits of degrees and that an agent may technically choose to participate while labouring under constraints so severe that the choice is nominal rather than genuine. The inductive generalisation from cases of cooperation among equals fails to accommodate the possibility that the form of cooperation may be preserved while its substance — genuine freedom of participation — is hollowed out by asymmetries of power.
passage:
The most perspicuous instances of cooperation in social life share a reassuring structure. Two farmers agree to harvest each other's fields in alternation, each contributing labour and each benefiting from the arrangement. Neighbours organise a snow-clearing rota, each household taking its turn for the benefit of all. Two scientists collaborate on a paper, each contributing expertise that the other lacks. In every case, the agents voluntarily coordinate their efforts toward a shared goal, each contributes something distinctive, and each benefits.

These cases invite the generalisation that cooperation consists in voluntary coordination toward a shared goal, with complementary contributions and mutual benefit. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from situations of severe power asymmetry. Suppose a powerful landlord offers starving tenants the following arrangement: they will work his fields collectively, pooling their labour toward the shared goal of bringing in the harvest, and each will receive a portion of the grain sufficient to survive the winter. The tenants coordinate their actions toward the shared goal; each contributes labour that the landlord cannot supply alone; and each benefits, since without the grain they would perish. All three conditions of the definition appear to be satisfied. Yet one might observe that the tenants' participation is not genuinely voluntary. They cooperate because the alternative is starvation, and the terms are dictated entirely by the landlord. What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that the agents enter the arrangement from positions of rough equality and genuine freedom of choice. The generalisation from cases of cooperation among equals breaks down when applied to arrangements in which the form of cooperation is preserved but its substance is hollowed out by severe asymmetries of power and the absence of genuine alternatives.

background_cases: The annual blessing of the fishing fleet in a coastal village has been performed continuously for three centuries. Each generation learns the ceremony from the preceding one, and the form of the ritual — the prayers, the procession to the harbour, the casting of flowers upon the water — has remained substantially unchanged. The villagers regard the ceremony as a cherished inheritance and perform it out of reverence for their ancestors and the continuity of their way of life.

The practice of afternoon tea in a certain country has been maintained for nearly two centuries. Each generation learns the customs — the order of service, the types of food, the social conventions — from the preceding one, and the practice has remained substantially similar throughout. Practitioners regard it as a cultural inheritance and observe it out of respect for a way of life they associate with their forebears.

The singing of a particular hymn at harvest festivals in a rural community has been passed down for over a hundred and fifty years. Each generation learns the hymn from the previous one, and the melody and words have remained unchanged. The community regards the hymn as an inheritance from their ancestors and sings it out of a sense of continuity with the past.
definition: A practice P constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted continuously from one generation to the next for a substantial period, (ii) the form of P has remained substantially unchanged across generations, and (iii) the practitioners of P perform it out of a sense of reverence for or continuity with the past.
conditions: P has been transmitted continuously from one generation to the next for a substantial period
The form of P has remained substantially unchanged across generations
The practitioners of P perform it out of a sense of reverence for or continuity with the past
counterexample: Consider the practice of constitutional government in a certain nation. The practice has been transmitted continuously across generations for over two centuries. Yet its form has changed substantially: the franchise has been extended, new branches of government created, and the balance of powers repeatedly altered. The practitioners — citizens and officials — perform their constitutional roles not out of a sense of reverence for or continuity with the past, but out of a belief in the ongoing legitimacy and practical effectiveness of the arrangement. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are not clearly satisfied. Yet many speakers would describe constitutional government in that nation as a tradition — indeed, as one of the most important traditions in its political life. The practice has the depth, continuity, and normative weight that we associate with traditions, even though its form has changed profoundly and its practitioners are motivated by present conviction rather than backward-looking reverence.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the practice in question is a discrete, bounded ceremony or custom whose form is easily preserved and whose motivating sentiment is explicitly retrospective. The definition captures this pattern faithfully. Yet the case of constitutional government reveals that some of the most significant practices we call traditions are complex, evolving, and forward-looking — their form changes substantially across generations, and their practitioners are motivated less by reverence for the past than by belief in the continuing value of the practice. The inductive generalisation from small-scale, ceremonial traditions to a universal account of tradition breaks down when confronted with large-scale institutional practices whose traditional character resides in continuity of commitment rather than in constancy of form or backward-looking sentiment.
passage:
The most recognisable instances of tradition share a reassuring structure. The annual blessing of a fishing fleet, performed continuously for three centuries with the same prayers and procession, is transmitted from generation to generation and performed out of reverence for ancestral ways. The practice of afternoon tea, maintained for nearly two hundred years in substantially the same form, is observed out of respect for a way of life associated with one's forebears. The singing of a particular harvest hymn, passed down for over a century and a half with melody and words unchanged, is performed from a sense of continuity with the past.

These cases invite the generalisation that a tradition is a practice transmitted continuously across generations, substantially unchanged in form, and performed out of reverence for or continuity with the past. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the domain of political life. Consider the practice of constitutional government in a certain nation. The practice has been transmitted continuously for over two centuries, and we might naturally describe it as one of the most important traditions in that nation's political life. Yet its form has changed profoundly: the franchise has been extended to classes and groups originally excluded, new institutions have been created, and the balance of governmental powers has been repeatedly revised. Moreover, citizens and officials do not typically perform their constitutional roles out of reverence for the past; they participate out of a present conviction in the legitimacy and practical effectiveness of the arrangement. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are not clearly satisfied. Yet one might observe that we should not deny the traditional character of this practice. What the background cases share — ceremonial fixity and backward-looking sentiment — represents only one species of tradition. The generalisation from small-scale, ceremonial practices to a universal account of tradition fails when confronted with large-scale institutional practices whose traditional character resides in continuity of commitment rather than in constancy of form or retrospective reverence.

background_cases: A man lends his house-key to a neighbour while he is away on business, confident that the neighbour will use it only to water the plants as agreed — the man is vulnerable, he believes in the neighbour's goodwill, and the relationship is personal.

A patient shares intimate details of her medical history with her doctor, confident that the doctor will use the information only for her treatment — the patient is vulnerable, she believes in the doctor's professional integrity, and the relationship involves direct personal interaction.

A woman leaves her child in the care of a trusted friend for an afternoon, confident that the friend will look after the child responsibly — the woman is deeply vulnerable, she believes in the friend's character, and the relationship is built upon years of mutual acquaintance.
definition: Agent A trusts agent B if and only if (i) A is vulnerable to B's actions, (ii) A believes that B possesses goodwill or competence sufficient to justify A's vulnerability, and (iii) the trust relation obtains within the context of a personal relationship between A and B.
conditions: A is vulnerable to B's actions
A believes that B possesses goodwill or competence sufficient to justify A's vulnerability
The trust relation obtains within the context of a personal relationship between A and B
counterexample: Consider a case in which a traveller boards an aeroplane piloted by a person she has never met and will never meet. She is profoundly vulnerable to the pilot's actions — her life depends upon his competence. She believes, on general grounds, that the pilot is trained, licensed, and competent. Yet there is no personal relationship between them whatever; she does not know his name, has never spoken to him, and has no acquaintance with his character. Condition (iii) fails, yet it seems entirely natural to say that she trusts the pilot — indeed, boarding the aeroplane would be irrational without some form of trust in the person at the controls.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the neighbour, the doctor, the friend — all involve trust relations embedded in personal acquaintance or direct professional interaction. The condition of a personal relationship was abstracted from a sample in which trust happened to be exercised between persons known to one another. But the case of the airline passenger reveals that trust extends to strangers through institutional mechanisms — training, certification, regulation — that serve as proxies for the personal knowledge of character that the background cases provide. The definition conflated the typical context in which trust is observed with a necessary condition of its occurrence.
passage:
When one considers instances of trust in everyday life, a pattern appears to emerge. The man who lends his house-key to a neighbour, the patient who confides in her doctor, the mother who leaves her child with a trusted friend — in each case, the person who trusts is vulnerable to the actions of another, believes that the other possesses sufficient goodwill or competence, and stands in a personal relationship with the person trusted. From these cases, one might infer that trust is a relation that obtains between persons who know one another, grounded in vulnerability, belief in the other's qualities, and the context of a personal connexion.

Yet this inference, plausible as it seems, is revealed to be too restrictive when one considers certain commonplace situations. A traveller arrives at an airport and boards an aeroplane. She takes her seat, fastens her belt, and entrusts her life — quite literally — to the skill and attentiveness of the pilot. She is profoundly vulnerable: should the pilot err, the consequences may be fatal. She believes, on grounds furnished by her general understanding of the airline industry, that the pilot is adequately trained and competent. And yet there is no personal relationship between them. She does not know the pilot's name; she has never seen his face; she has no acquaintance with his character, his habits, or his history. Condition (iii) is plainly unsatisfied. But it would be very strange to deny that the traveller trusts the pilot. Her willingness to board the aircraft is intelligible only on the assumption that she places some measure of trust in the person at the controls. The most satisfying explanation of why the definition fails here is that the background cases all involved trust between persons known to one another, and the condition of a personal relationship was abstracted from this coincidence. In truth, trust extends to complete strangers through the mediation of institutional mechanisms — professional training, governmental certification, regulatory oversight — that serve as substitutes for the personal acquaintance upon which the background cases relied.

background_cases: Two farmers agree to help one another bring in the harvest — each contributes labour on the other's land, and both benefit from the arrangement.
Members of a village jointly build a well that will serve the entire community — each contributes effort, and all share in the benefit of clean water.
A group of fishermen coordinate their boats to encircle a shoal of fish — each plays an assigned role, and the catch is divided among them.
definition: An interaction between agents is an instance of cooperation if and only if (i) the agents coordinate their actions toward a shared goal, (ii) each agent contributes effort that is necessary for the achievement of the goal, and (iii) the agents communicate or agree upon a plan of action prior to acting.
conditions: The agents coordinate their actions toward a shared goal
Each agent contributes effort that is necessary for the achievement of the goal
The agents communicate or agree upon a plan of action prior to acting
counterexample: Consider a case in which several motorists arrive at a congested roundabout simultaneously. Without any communication or prior agreement, each adjusts his speed and position in response to the movements of the others, and the result is a smooth flow of traffic through the junction. Their actions are coordinated toward a shared goal — the efficient passage of all vehicles — and each motorist's careful driving contributes effort necessary for avoiding a collision. Yet condition (iii) is not satisfied: there has been no communication and no prior agreement upon a plan. The coordination emerges spontaneously from the independent responses of the individual agents. Yet the smooth functioning of the roundabout is commonly cited as an instance of social cooperation.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the farmers, the villagers, the fishermen — all involve explicit communication and prior agreement. The definition abstracted this feature as a necessary condition of cooperation. But the case of the roundabout reveals that cooperation can emerge without any communication or prior plan, through the spontaneous mutual adjustment of independent agents responding to shared circumstances. The inductive generalisation mistook a common but contingent feature of the sample for a necessary condition of cooperation as such.
passage:
The cases of the farmers who exchange labour, the villagers who build a well together, and the fishermen who coordinate their boats exhibit a common structure. In each, the agents communicate, agree upon a plan, coordinate their actions toward a shared end, and each contributes effort without which the goal could not be achieved. It is natural to infer from these instances that cooperation requires not only coordinated action and mutual contribution but also prior communication and agreement upon a plan.

Yet consider the scene at a busy roundabout during the morning rush. A dozen motorists, strangers to one another, arrive at the junction from several directions at roughly the same moment. No one has communicated with anyone else; there has been no conference, no signal, no agreement of any kind. Yet each driver adjusts his speed and trajectory in response to the movements of the others — yielding here, accelerating there, merging with a deftness that, viewed from above, appears almost choreographed. The result is a smooth and orderly flow of traffic, each vehicle passing through the junction without mishap. The drivers' actions are coordinated toward a shared goal — the efficient and safe passage of all — and each driver's careful conduct is a contribution without which the collective achievement would fail; a single reckless motorist would bring the system to ruin. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. But condition (iii) is not: there has been no communication, no prior agreement, no plan. The coordination has emerged spontaneously from the independent responses of a number of agents to a shared situation. And yet the smooth functioning of the roundabout is, by common consent, an instance of cooperation — indeed, it is frequently cited as a model of how social order can arise without central direction. The most plausible explanation of the definition's failure is that the background cases all happened to involve cooperation preceded by explicit communication. The generalisation to condition (iii) was an artefact of the sample, not a necessary feature of cooperation. Cooperation, it appears, can arise spontaneously through mutual adjustment, without any prior agreement or exchange of intentions.

background_cases: In a particular society, it is the norm that guests remove their shoes upon entering a home — virtually everyone does so, everyone expects it, and a guest who fails to remove her shoes is met with visible disapproval.

In a university department, it is the norm that junior members address senior members by their title and surname — the practice is universal, the expectation is well understood, and violations provoke mild but unmistakable censure.

In a certain profession, it is the norm that members do not publicly criticise one another's work — compliance is near-universal, the expectation is explicitly communicated to new members, and those who violate it are subjected to informal ostracism.
definition: A rule R is a social norm within a group G if and only if (i) R is complied with by the large majority of members of G, (ii) members of G are aware of R and expect one another to comply, and (iii) R has been in force within G for a significant period of time.
conditions: R is complied with by the large majority of members of G
Members of G are aware of R and expect one another to comply
R has been in force within G for a significant period of time
counterexample: Consider a case in which a new social media platform is launched, and within a matter of days a norm emerges among its users: one must not share another user's personal photograph without permission. Within a week, the large majority of users comply with this rule; the expectation is widely discussed and understood; and those who violate it are met with sharp criticism. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. But condition (iii) is not: the norm has existed for only a few days. It has not been in force for a significant period of time by any ordinary standard. Yet it functions as a norm in every other respect — it shapes behaviour, structures expectations, and provides grounds for criticism. The case reveals that temporal duration is not essential to the existence of a norm.
abductive_insight: The background cases — shoe-removal, forms of address, professional discretion — are all established norms with histories extending over years or decades. The temporal condition was abstracted from a sample of mature norms. But the social media case reveals that norms can crystallise with extraordinary rapidity when a community forms around new circumstances. The duration of a norm is a common feature of the instances from which the definition was drawn but not a necessary condition for a rule to function as a norm. The definition confused the typical life-cycle of norms with their essential character.
passage:
Surveying the norms of shoe-removal in private homes, of formal address in academic settings, and of professional discretion among colleagues, one observes a common pattern: each norm is complied with by the majority, each is well understood and mutually expected, and each has been in force for a substantial period. From these observations, one might infer that a social norm is a rule that enjoys widespread compliance, mutual awareness, and a significant history within the group. The temporal condition — that the norm must have been in force for some appreciable time — seems a natural requirement, for it distinguishes genuine norms from fleeting fashions or momentary agreements.

Yet consider the following case, which places considerable pressure upon the third condition. A new social media platform is launched, attracting millions of users within its first week. Almost immediately — within a matter of days — a norm emerges: users are not to share another person's photograph without that person's express permission. This rule is not imposed by the platform's operators; it arises spontaneously among the users themselves. Within a week, the large majority comply; the expectation is widely discussed in the platform's forums; and users who violate the rule are met with vigorous criticism, their reputations visibly damaged. The rule functions, in every respect that matters, as a social norm: it shapes behaviour, structures expectations, furnishes grounds for censure, and is recognised by the community as binding. Yet it has existed for only a few days. Condition (iii) is not satisfied — the norm has no significant history. The most compelling explanation of this failure is that the background cases were all drawn from well-established communities with long-standing norms, and the temporal condition was abstracted from the accidental longevity of the sample. In truth, norms can crystallise with remarkable speed when a new community forms around novel circumstances, and their normative force does not depend upon the passage of time but upon the presence of mutual expectation and the social mechanisms that enforce compliance.

definition: Trust between persons obtains if and only if both parties have a personal relationship of mutual acquaintance, both parties have observed each other's behaviour over an extended period, and both parties have reciprocated acts of goodwill.
conditions: Both parties have a personal relationship of mutual acquaintance
Both parties have observed each other's behaviour over an extended period
Both parties have reciprocated acts of goodwill
background_cases: Two neighbours who have lived side by side for many years, observed each other's behaviour daily, and regularly exchanged favours trust one another to watch over each other's property.
Two colleagues who have worked together for a decade, observed each other's professional conduct, and reciprocated acts of professional support trust one another with sensitive information.

Two childhood friends who have known each other since youth, observed each other's character through many trials, and consistently helped one another in times of difficulty trust each other implicitly.
counterexample: Consider a case in which a traveller in a foreign country flags down a passing motorist and asks for directions to the nearest town. The traveller trusts the motorist's directions and follows them. The two have no personal relationship of mutual acquaintance, have never observed each other's behaviour, and have exchanged no acts of goodwill. Yet the traveller genuinely trusts the motorist — trusts that the directions are accurate and that no deception is intended. This trust is grounded not in personal history but in a general presumption of human helpfulness and honesty in low-stakes encounters.
abductive_insight: The background cases, all involving long-standing personal relationships, encourage the inference that trust requires mutual acquaintance, sustained observation, and reciprocated goodwill. But the traveller case reveals that trust can arise instantaneously between strangers on the basis of general social presumptions and contextual cues. Our induction from cases of deep personal trust has caused us to overlook the pervasive phenomenon of default trust among strangers, which operates without any of the conditions we abstracted from our sample.
passage:
When one considers the most salient examples of trust between persons, they tend to involve deep and long-standing personal relationships. Two neighbours who have lived side by side for many years, observing each other's daily conduct and regularly exchanging small favours, come to trust one another with the security of their homes. Two colleagues who have collaborated for a decade, witnessing each other's professional integrity and reciprocating acts of support, trust one another with sensitive information. Two childhood friends who have known each other through many trials and have consistently aided one another in adversity trust each other with a depth that seems unshakeable. From such examples one might inductively conclude that trust between persons obtains when both have a personal relationship of mutual acquaintance, have observed each other's behaviour over a long period, and have reciprocated acts of goodwill.

Yet we should hesitate to accept this generalisation as exhaustive. Suppose that a traveller in a foreign country, knowing no one and having arrived only that morning, flags down a passing motorist and asks for directions to the nearest town. The motorist points down a road and offers brief instructions. The traveller follows them without hesitation, trusting that the directions are accurate and that no deception is intended. Yet the traveller and the motorist have no personal relationship whatsoever; they have never met, have never observed each other's behaviour, and have exchanged no acts of goodwill. The trust is instantaneous and unilateral, grounded not in personal history but in a general presumption of human helpfulness and honesty in ordinary, low-stakes encounters. All three conditions of our inductively derived definition are unsatisfied, yet trust plainly obtains. Our generalisation from cases of deep personal trust has thus caused us to overlook the pervasive and important phenomenon of default trust among strangers, which operates on quite different foundations.

definition: A behavioural regularity constitutes a social norm if and only if it is explicitly taught to new members of the group, compliance is publicly observable, and violators are subject to verbal censure by other members.
conditions: The regularity is explicitly taught to new members of the group
Compliance is publicly observable
Violators are subject to verbal censure by other members
background_cases: The norm of queuing in an orderly line is explicitly taught to children, compliance is publicly observable in shops and bus stops, and those who push ahead are subject to verbal reproach.

The norm of greeting acquaintances by name is explicitly taught in childhood, compliance is publicly observable in social encounters, and failure to greet is met with verbal censure or pointed remarks.
The norm of removing one's hat indoors is explicitly taught to young people, compliance is publicly observable upon entering a building, and those who keep their hats on are verbally corrected.
counterexample: Consider a case in which, within a particular professional community, there exists a norm against discussing one's salary with colleagues. This norm is never explicitly taught; new members absorb it through observation and subtle cues, inferring from the discomfort that arises when the topic is broached. Compliance is not publicly observable, since the norm concerns the absence of a behaviour — specifically, the absence of salary discussion — and the absence of a conversation is not readily detected by observers. Moreover, violators are not subject to verbal censure; rather, they are met with awkward silence, changed subjects, and a gradual social cooling. Yet the norm against salary discussion is robust and widely recognised. It powerfully shapes behaviour within the community.
abductive_insight: The background cases feature norms that are explicitly taught, publicly observable, and enforced through verbal censure, tempting the inference that all social norms share these properties. But the salary-silence norm reveals that norms may be tacitly absorbed, concern unobservable omissions, and be enforced through non-verbal social mechanisms such as awkwardness and exclusion. Induction from salient, overt norms yields a picture that systematically excludes the large class of tacit norms governing what is not said and not done.
passage:
The social norms that most readily come to mind are those whose operation is conspicuous and whose enforcement is direct. The norm of queuing is explicitly taught to children from an early age, compliance is publicly observable at every shop counter and bus stop, and those who violate it by pushing ahead are subject to verbal reproach from fellow queue-members. The norm of greeting an acquaintance by name is similarly instilled through childhood instruction, its observance plain to all present, and its neglect met with pointed remarks or expressions of displeasure. The norm of removing one's hat upon entering a building is explicitly communicated to young people, compliance is immediately visible, and violators are verbally corrected. From these cases one might infer that a behavioural regularity constitutes a social norm when it is explicitly taught to newcomers, when compliance is publicly observable, and when violators are subject to verbal censure.

Yet we should hesitate to rest upon this generalisation. Consider the norm, prevalent in many professional communities, against discussing one's salary with colleagues. This norm is never explicitly taught to new members of the organisation; rather, it is absorbed through observation and inference, as newcomers notice the discomfort that attends any approach to the topic. Compliance with the norm is not publicly observable in any straightforward sense, for the norm concerns the absence of a type of conversation, and the absence of a discussion is not a phenomenon that readily presents itself to observation. Moreover, violators are not subject to verbal censure; instead, they are met with awkward silences, abruptly changed subjects, and a subtle but unmistakable social cooling. Yet the norm is robust and widely recognised within the community, powerfully shaping the behaviour of its members. This case reveals that our induction from salient, overt norms has yielded too narrow a conception, one that excludes the large and important class of tacit norms governing what is not said and not done, enforced not by verbal censure but by the quiet withdrawal of social ease.

definition: Agents cooperate if and only if they communicate their intentions to one another beforehand, they coordinate their actions according to an agreed plan, and each agent sacrifices some individual advantage for the sake of the collective outcome.
conditions: The agents communicate their intentions to one another beforehand
They coordinate their actions according to an agreed plan
Each agent sacrifices some individual advantage for the sake of the collective outcome
background_cases: A group of villagers agrees to build a bridge, each communicating what tasks they will perform, coordinating their labour according to a plan, and sacrificing leisure time for the collective benefit.

A string quartet communicates before a performance about tempo and dynamics, coordinates according to the score and agreed interpretation, and each player subordinates individual expression to the ensemble's sound.

A team of firefighters communicates a plan of attack before entering a burning building, coordinates their movements according to that plan, and each accepts personal risk for the collective goal of saving lives.
counterexample: Suppose that during a severe winter, every household in a village independently clears the snow from the stretch of public road in front of its own property. No communication occurs between the households; each acts from pure self-interest, wishing to be able to leave its own driveway. No plan is agreed upon, and no household sacrifices any individual advantage — each benefits directly and immediately from its own clearing. Yet the cumulative result is a fully cleared village road, a collective good that benefits everyone. An observer would describe the villagers as having cooperated to clear the road, though none of the three conditions is satisfied.
abductive_insight: The background cases, all involving deliberate coordination with communication, planning, and sacrifice, encourage the inference that cooperation necessarily involves these features. But the snow-clearing case shows that cooperation can emerge from the uncoordinated, self-interested actions of individuals when those actions happen to be complementary. This reveals a form of cooperation that is invisible to an analysis derived from paradigm cases of deliberate collective action: emergent cooperation, in which the cooperative outcome arises without any agent intending or arranging it.
passage:
The cases that most readily suggest themselves as examples of cooperation share a common and seemingly essential structure. When villagers agree to build a bridge, they communicate their intentions, coordinate their labour according to an agreed plan, and each sacrifices leisure time for the collective benefit. When the members of a string quartet prepare for a performance, they discuss matters of tempo and phrasing, coordinate their playing according to the score and a shared interpretation, and each musician subordinates individual expression to the ensemble's collective sound. When firefighters enter a burning building, they communicate a plan of attack, coordinate their movements according to that plan, and each accepts personal risk for the shared goal of saving lives. From such paradigm cases one might inductively conclude that cooperation requires prior communication of intentions, coordination according to an agreed plan, and individual sacrifice for the collective outcome.

Yet we should hesitate to extend this conclusion beyond its evidential base. Suppose that during a severe winter, every household in a village independently clears the snow from the stretch of public road fronting its own property. No household communicates with any other about this activity; each acts from a purely self-interested desire to be able to leave its own driveway without obstruction. No plan is agreed upon or even contemplated. And no household sacrifices any individual advantage, since each benefits directly and immediately from clearing its own section. Yet the cumulative result is a fully cleared village road — a collective good that benefits every resident, including those who might not have been able to clear the road alone. An observer would naturally describe the villagers as having cooperated to clear the road, though not one of the three conditions derived from our background cases is satisfied. This reveals a form of cooperation invisible to our inductively derived analysis: emergent cooperation, in which a cooperative outcome arises from the convergence of independent, self-interested actions without communication, planning, or sacrifice.
